title: crustc: entirety of `rustc`, translated to C
blurb: crustc is a complete C translation of the Rust compiler that builds with GCC and make, then compiles core, alloc, and std like the real rustc. It is a teaser for cilly, a Rust-to-C backend that generates compiler-specific C by probing each target's type layouts, alignments, and keyword support, aiming to bring Rust to old and obscure hardware that LLVM and GCC never targeted.

title: FBI Seizes NetNut Proxy Platform, Popa Botnet
blurb: The FBI seized hundreds of domains tied to NetNut, a residential proxy service run by publicly traded Israeli firm Alarum Technologies, two weeks after security researchers linked it to the two-million-device Popa botnet. Google, Lumen, and Shadowserver aided the takedown, with Google's threat group reporting 316 distinct threat-actor clusters using NetNut exit nodes in a single week of June to mask malicious traffic.

title: CISA: Microsoft SharePoint RCE flaw now actively exploited
blurb: CISA warned that attackers are actively exploiting CVE-2026-45659, a high-severity SharePoint remote code execution flaw that lets any low-privileged authenticated user run arbitrary code on unpatched servers. Microsoft issued patches on May 21 for SharePoint 2016, 2019, and Subscription Edition, but Shadowserver still tracks over 10,000 exposed servers, and CISA has ordered federal agencies to patch by Saturday.

title: Podman v6.0.0
blurb: Podman v6.0.0 modernizes the container tool's networking stack, transitioning from slirp4netns and iptables to Netavark, Pasta, and nftables while overhauling Quadlets with REST API support. The release also adds a new podman machine os update command, refines multi-provider VM support, and updates Docker API compatibility to smooth migrations off Docker.

title: Is One Layer Enough? A Single Transformer Layer Matches Full-Parameter RL Train
blurb: A new paper finds that training a single transformer layer can recover most of the gains from full-parameter RL post-training, and sometimes beats it. Across seven Qwen models, three RL algorithms, and tasks spanning math, code, and agentic decision-making, the authors show RL gains concentrate in a few middle layers, with input and output layers contributing far less.

title: AI Writes Faster Than Humans Can Review: A Longitudinal Study of an Enterprise 2x Mandate
blurb: A longitudinal study of 802 developers and 196,212 pull requests found that an enterprise "2x" mandate to double merged PRs per engineer eventually hit 2.09x baseline throughput by April 2026, driven by AI coding tool adoption rather than the mandate itself. Per-reviewer load roughly doubled and automated review overtook human review, while merge and revert rates held steady.

title: Since Linux 6.9, LUKS suspend stopped wiping disk-encryption keys from memory
blurb: A regression introduced in Linux 6.9 broke LUKS "suspend," which is supposed to evict disk-encryption keys from RAM so a locked laptop can't be trivially decrypted. Since that release the keys have silently stayed in memory, meaning users who relied on the feature for at-rest protection were exposed without any warning for over a year.

title: Vite+ Beta
blurb: Vite+ entered beta as a unified web toolchain that bundles Vite, Vitest, Rolldown, tsdown, Oxlint, and Oxfmt behind a single `vp` command with a monorepo-aware task runner and caching. The MIT-licensed, framework-agnostic stack aims to replace hand-assembled toolchains, keeping tool versions aligned across a team so CI runs the same commands developers use locally.

title: CursorBench 3.1
blurb: Cursor released CursorBench 3.1, an eval that scores coding agents on ambiguous, multi-file tasks drawn from real Cursor sessions. The new version adds problems targeting codebase understanding, bugfinding, planning, and code review, and reports average cost per task by applying each model's published token pricing to the tokens it used.

title: Smooth AI criminal drives 'first' end-to-end agentic ransomware attack
blurb: Security researchers documented what they call the first end-to-end agentic ransomware attack, in which an AI system autonomously handled reconnaissance, intrusion, and encryption with minimal human direction. The case marks a shift from AI assisting individual attack steps to orchestrating the full kill chain, lowering the skill and time needed to run a campaign and signaling faster, cheaper ransomware operations ahead.

title: Program-as-Weights: A Programming Paradigm for Fuzzy Functions
blurb: This paper proposes fuzzy-function programming, which compiles a natural-language spec into a small, locally-executable neural artifact instead of calling a large LLM API per request. Their Program-as-Weights system uses a 4B compiler to emit adapters for a frozen 0.6B Qwen3 interpreter that matches Qwen3-32B prompting at roughly one fiftieth the inference memory, running at 30 tokens/s on a MacBook M3.

title: Apple Hide My Email bug seemingly allows 100% of real email addresses to be discovered
blurb: A security researcher disclosed a flaw in Apple's Hide My Email that let attackers reveal the real address behind every generated alias, with tests reportedly succeeding on 100% of addresses. EasyOptOuts co-founder Tyler Murphy said he reported the bug to Apple over a year ago and went public after repeated missed fix deadlines, and 404 Media confirmed the vulnerability remains exploitable.

title: Microsoft pours $2.5B in push to embed engineers with customers
blurb: Microsoft will spend $2.5B to deploy 6,000 engineers who embed directly with customers through its new Frontier Company unit. The move joins a broader big tech push to staff up on-the-ground support for AI deployment, signaling that the hard part has shifted from building models to getting customers to run them in production.

title: Controllable Sim Agents with Behavior Latents
blurb: Researchers introduced CNeVA, a traffic-simulation framework that generates realistic agent behavior while letting engineers steer it along interpretable axes like speed, acceleration, and safety. On the Waymo Open Motion Dataset, the model matches competitive realism while exposing per-channel controllability that higher-ranked imitation models lack, giving autonomous-vehicle teams a way to reproduce specific edge cases without real-world risk.

title: Politician who investigated spyware abuses had his phone hacked with Pegasus spyware
blurb: Citizen Lab confirmed that Greek journalist and former MEP Stelios Kouloglou was hacked with NSO Group's Pegasus spyware in 2022 and 2023 while serving on the European Parliament committee investigating spyware abuse, the first PEGA committee member publicly identified as a victim. Researchers linked the attack to a government customer that reused a Pegasus email address from earlier campaigns against European journalists, though no specific country was named.

title: Startup activates nuclear microreactor live on stage to power an Nvidia RTX Spark desktop PC — firm working with Nvidia to build a 30MW closed loop AI factory that doesn’t use local water
blurb: Valar Atomics activated its Ward 250 nuclear microreactor live on stage, running it to 37% power to drive an Nvidia RTX Blackwell PC that served a live website. The startup is partnering with Nvidia on a 30MW closed-loop AI factory that avoids local water use, joining a nuclear-for-data-centers push that already includes reactors from Deployable Energy and Antares Nuclear.

title: AgenticSTS: A Bounded-Memory Testbed for Long-Horizon LLM Agents
blurb: AgenticSTS is a bounded-memory testbed that studies long-horizon LLM agents in Slay the Spire 2, releasing 298 tagged trajectories and analysis scripts.

title: Crunchbase Data: Global Startup Investment Hit Record $510B In H1 2026 As AI Boom Accelerates Funding And Exits
blurb: Global venture funding hit a record $510B in H1 2026, surpassing all of 2025's $440B and topping the previous half-year peak set in 2021, according to Crunchbase. OpenAI and Anthropic alone took $217B, 43% of the total, while Q2 also produced the strongest exit market in years, headlined by SpaceX's $1.77T IPO and its $60B acquisition of Cursor maker Anysphere.

title: Vulnerability and Malware Checks in uv
blurb: Astral added two preview security features to uv: a native `uv audit` command for vulnerability scanning and opt-in OSV malware checks.

title: Adoption and Impact of Command-Line AI Coding Agents: A Study of Microsoft's Early 2026 Rollout of Claude Code and GitHub Copilot CLI
blurb: A study of tens of thousands of Microsoft engineers during the early-2026 rollout of Claude Code and GitHub Copilot CLI found that adopters merged roughly 24% more pull requests, with the lift persisting across a four-month window. First use spread mainly through peer networks and retention tracked coding activity rather than demographics, leading the authors to recommend that organizations treat visible peer use as central to rollout strategy.

title: Bayesian Sparse Low-Rank Adaptation for Large Language Model Uncertainty Estimation
blurb: DALorRA applies variational Bayesian masking to LoRA's rank dimensions, calibrating fine-tuned LLM confidence without hurting reasoning accuracy.

title: Ransomware Groups Turn to Citrix Bleed 2, BYOVD, and Supply Chain Credentials
blurb: Anubis ransomware affiliates are exploiting Citrix Bleed 2 (CVE-2025-5777) and legitimate RMM tools to gain access and spread laterally.

title: Regression Accumulation in Multi-Turn LLM Programming Conversations
blurb: Across six LLMs, 40% to 73% of multi-turn coding tasks lose previously correct behavior; a verification-gate rollback is the only fix that helps all models.

title: Decoupling Code Complexity from Newcomer Participation: A Causal Study of AI Coding Agent Adoption in OSS
blurb: A causal study of 1,888 open-source projects finds that adopting AI coding agents like Cursor and Claude Code does not crowd out newcomer contributors, with no significant decline in inflow, onboarding, or retention after adoption. Using difference-in-differences against matched controls, the authors show that while agents raise per-function code complexity by roughly 11% on a Python cognitive metric, newcomer participation held steady even in the subsets where complexity rose.

title: Anthropic's AI Finds Bugs. IBM Bets $5B It Can Fix Them.
blurb: IBM and Red Hat committed $5B to Project Lightwell, a subscription patching service for enterprises running business-critical open-source software they can't safely update in production. The bet responds to Anthropic's Mythos model, which has disclosed 1,596 vetted vulnerabilities across 281 projects while maintainers patched only about 6%, exposing a supply-chain gap between AI-speed discovery and human-speed fixes.

title: Robinhood Chain Mainnet Goes Live With Stock Tokens
blurb: Robinhood launched Robinhood Chain, a public Arbitrum-based Layer 2, alongside stock tokens that track real shares held 1:1 by the broker and can be used as DeFi collateral in 120+ countries. The suite also adds ~7% USDG lending on Morpho and leveraged commodity, ETF, and FX perpetuals in Europe, but excludes the US, where Robinhood is still awaiting an SEC framework for tokenized equity.

title: New Alibaba AI framework skips loading every tool, cutting agent token use 99%
blurb: Alibaba researchers released SkillWeaver, a framework that builds an execution graph for a task and routes each node to the right skill instead of loading an agent's entire tool library into context. Its Skill-Aware Decomposition technique iteratively fetches and vets tool candidates through a feedback loop, raising accuracy while cutting token consumption by over 99% versus exposing every tool at once.

title: Someone infected a spyware probe overseer with spyware
blurb: Citizen Lab found NSO Group's Pegasus spyware on the phone of Stelios Kouloglou, a substitute member of the European Parliament committee that investigated spyware abuses, marking the first known infection of a PEGA Committee member. The phone was compromised twice in 2022 and 2023 as the panel drafted its final report, underscoring that the committee's own recommendations to curb spyware misuse were never enacted.

title: Microsoft launches AI-focused professional services business with $2.5B investment
blurb: Microsoft launched the Microsoft Frontier Company, a professional services business with an initial $2.5B investment and 6,000 engineers to help enterprises build and manage custom AI applications. Led by former Microsoft Asia president Rodrigo Kede Lima, the unit sends forward-deployed engineers into client offices and partners with Accenture, Capgemini, and EY, mirroring a consulting model pioneered by frontier labs like OpenAI.

title: Microsoft launches $2.5 billion Frontier Company to help enterprises move beyond AI pilots
blurb: Microsoft launched Frontier Company, a $2.5B unit that helps enterprises move AI projects from pilots into production. The group provides hands-on implementation and enterprise-grade execution rather than raw models, reflecting Microsoft's bet that the industry bottleneck has shifted from model quality to real-world deployment.

title: DemoPSD: Disagreement-Modulated Policy Self-Distillation
blurb: DemoPSD trains reasoning LLMs by selectively blending teacher and student token distributions, cutting privileged-information leakage while outperforming GRPO and SDPO on scientific benchmarks.

title: Lightning Memory-Mapped Database Manager (LMDB) 1.0
blurb: LMDB 1.0 released, a memory-mapped, fully-ACID embedded key/value store that returns data straight from the mapped memory with no caching layer.

title: Redeploying Fable 5
blurb: Anthropic restored access to Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5 after the US government lifted export controls that had forced a three-week suspension over concerns about cyber-misuse of the models. Fable 5 returns globally across Claude.ai, Claude Code, and Claude Cowork, while Mythos 5 is limited to approved US organizations as Anthropic and Glasswing partners develop a shared industry framework for assessing model jailbreaks.

title: iOS 27 helps apps detect when a user may be getting scammed in real time
blurb: Apple is adding a framework called Trust Insights to iOS 27 that lets apps detect in real time when a user may be getting coached through a social engineering scam. The system runs mostly on-device, analyzing interaction patterns, timing, and sensor data across payments, account changes, and messaging without inspecting the contents of Photos, Messages, or Mail, then returns a risk level apps can act on with warnings or added verification.

title: UK dilutes stablecoin capital requirement in final crypto rulebook
blurb: The UK softened stablecoin capital requirements in its final crypto rulebook, easing reserve rules for issuers after industry pushback.

title: Sources: Alexandr Wang said Meta's model currently in training, codenamed Watermelon, matches GPT-5.5 and uses an "order of magnitude more compute than Avocado" (Business Insider)
blurb: Meta's next model, codenamed Watermelon and currently in training, matches GPT-5.5 and uses an order of magnitude more compute than its predecessor Avocado, according to sources citing Alexandr Wang. The disclosure signals Meta is closing the frontier gap after Wang took over its superintelligence effort, betting heavily on scaled compute to catch OpenAI.

title: Immich 3.0
blurb: Immich, the open-source self-hosted photo and video backup platform, released version 3.0.0, its latest major update. The project positions itself as a privacy-focused alternative to Google Photos, letting users store and manage their libraries on their own hardware rather than a cloud provider.

title: Virginia bans sale of geolocation data
blurb: Virginia enacted S.B. 388, amending its Consumer Data Protection Act to ban the sale of geolocation data starting July 1, 2026. Virginia joins Maryland and Oregon with such bans, though its narrower definition of "sale" covers only exchanges for monetary consideration, and California, Massachusetts, Vermont, and Washington have proposed similar restrictions.

title: Visa, Stripe among firms joining forces on mainstream stablecoin
blurb: Visa and Stripe are joining a group of financial and tech firms to build a mainstream stablecoin aimed at everyday payments. The consortium bets that a jointly issued token can bring stablecoins into routine commerce, positioning incumbents against crypto-native issuers like Circle and Tether as regulatory clarity improves.

title: Vercel's Andrew Qu on why agents are a new kind of software
blurb: In an interview with Latent Space, Vercel's Andrew Qu describes how the company built eve, its framework for agents, after hitting paper cuts that existing tooling ignored: switching models mid-run, adding fallbacks, and making runs resumable. Qu argues agents are a distinct kind of software, and that primitives like skills, sandboxes, filesystem access, compaction, and subagents should ship out of the box rather than being rebuilt each time.

title: Microsoft launches its own AI deployment company with $2.5 billion commitment
blurb: Microsoft launched Microsoft Frontier Company, a new business backed by a $2.5B investment and 6,000 engineers to run enterprise AI deployments using Microsoft's existing tools. The move follows FDE-style ventures from AWS, OpenAI, and Anthropic, but Microsoft's head start with much of the Fortune 500 gives it early partners including the London Stock Exchange Group, Unilever, and Accenture.

title: Chain-of-Thought Spoofing Targets Reasoning AI Models
blurb: Researchers demonstrate CoT Forgery, an attack that spoofs an LLM's trusted reasoning by mimicking its internal think-style over role tags.

title: Sources: Anthropic moves to close loopholes that let Chinese firms like Ant use its models via workarounds including cloud providers and overseas subsidiaries (Financial Times)
blurb: Anthropic is moving to close loopholes that let Chinese firms including Ant Group access its models through workarounds like third-party cloud providers and overseas subsidiaries, according to the Financial Times. The crackdown tightens enforcement of Anthropic's existing policy barring companies majority-owned by Chinese entities, reflecting growing pressure to keep frontier US models out of restricted markets.

title: Kling Raises $2.8 Billion Amid Planned Spinoff From Kuaishou
blurb: Kuaishou's AI video unit Kling raised $2.8B as the Chinese short-video company prepares to spin it off and list it separately. The round underscores how much capital investors are directing toward AI video generation, a market where Kling competes with tools like OpenAI's Sora and Google's Veo.

title: How OpenAI Delivers Low-Latency Voice AI for 900M Users
blurb: OpenAI runs real-time voice for 900M weekly users on WebRTC, splitting the stack into a stateless edge relay that routes packets and a stateful transceiver that owns the heavy connection state. The relay reads the ICE ufrag off a session's first packet to pick a backend, which sidesteps the problem that Kubernetes treats the stable IPs and ports WebRTC expects as disposable.

title: Meta’s push into cloud computing means Wall Street has to prepare for lower margins
blurb: Meta will sell excess computing power to outside customers, moving into a cloud infrastructure market dominated by Amazon, Microsoft, and Google. The company is weighing whether to offer hosted AI models or raw compute, a bet that diversifies revenue but carries far thinner margins than its core ad business. Meta's stock jumped 9% on the news, its sharpest rally in over five months.

title: Nvidia offers to take a cut of AI cloud revenue on top of hardware sales in new optional financing vehicle — trades tokens for revenue cut
blurb: Nvidia introduced a "revenue-sharing and credit-support" financing model that lets it collect a recurring cut of AI cloud income on top of the hardware it already sells. Cash-poor AI clouds get Nvidia GPU credits today in exchange for a slice of future revenue, with Australia's Sharon AI and Singapore's Firmus as the first partners across a combined 210,000 GPUs. Split percentages were not disclosed.

title: Claude Fable relaunch disappoints users with nerfed performance
blurb: Anthropic made Claude Fable 5 available to all Max, Pro, and Team subscribers, but early users report the restored model feels weaker and frequently falls back to Opus 4.8 on coding and security-adjacent tasks. Access is capped at 50% of weekly usage limits and shifts entirely to pay-per-use credits after July 7, while BleepingComputer attributes the degraded behavior to stricter safety guardrails rather than a downgraded model.

title: Microsoft launches AI engineering company
blurb: Microsoft launched the Microsoft Frontier Company, a new operating business backed by a $2.5B investment and 6,000 engineering and industry experts embedded with customers to design, deploy, and improve their AI systems. The unit pushes Microsoft further into hands-on AI services, betting that enterprises need embedded engineering help to move deployments past pilots and into production.

title: Trump gets OpenAI to offer US 5% stake, far lower than Sanders’ target
blurb: OpenAI is in early-stage talks with the Trump administration about handing the US government a 5% stake in the company, according to the Financial Times. Sam Altman argues giving the public a financial share is the best way to spread AI's upside, and the administration has floated similar deals with Google and Meta as it courts firms amid rising anti-AI sentiment.

title: Alibaba, Tencent Join $2.8 Billion Round for Kling AI
blurb: Alibaba and Tencent joined a $2.8B financing round for Kuaishou's Kling AI, valuing the Chinese generative video service at about $15B before the investment. The round pulls two of China's largest tech companies behind a rival to OpenAI's Sora and Google's Veo, signaling a domestic push to compete in AI video generation.

title: ElevenLabs in Talks on Tender Offer at $22 Billion Valuation
blurb: ElevenLabs is in early talks with investors on a tender offer that would let employees sell shares at a roughly $22 billion valuation. The secondary sale would more than double the AI voice startup's last reported valuation and give staff liquidity without a traditional funding round or exit.

title: Greek Politician Investigating Spyware Had Mobile Phone Hacked
blurb: New research found that spyware repeatedly hacked the phone of a Greek politician while he led a European Parliament investigation into the surveillance-tech vendors themselves. The case underscores how commercial spyware is turned against the officials tasked with regulating it, deepening questions about oversight and accountability across the EU's fragmented surveillance market.

title: Microsoft commits $2.5 billion and 6,000 employees to new AI implementation unit
blurb: Microsoft committed $2.5B and 6,000 employees to Microsoft Frontier Co., a new unit that embeds forward-deployed engineers with customers to run their AI implementations. The move follows Amazon's $1B FDE initiative two days earlier and mirrors similar groups at Anthropic and OpenAI, signaling that the industry's bottleneck has shifted from model quality to enterprise adoption.

title: Read the Emails Revealing How Anthropic’s Pentagon Relationship Fell Apart
blurb: The Wall Street Journal published emails showing how Anthropic's relationship with the Pentagon deteriorated over months of disputes about AI safety guardrails. Undersecretary Emil Michael and CEO Dario Amodei exchanged messages debating the limits Anthropic placed on military use of its models, exposing the friction between frontier labs' usage restrictions and the defense contracts they are pursuing.

title: Enterprises lost Claude Fable 5 for a few weeks. New data shows two-thirds had already built their hedge
blurb: New VentureBeat Pulse Research of 145 enterprises found that two-thirds had already hedged their AI model strategy before a June 12 export-control order pulled Anthropic's Claude Fable 5 offline for every customer. 51% now blend closed frontier models with open-weight models on their own infrastructure and another 16% are moving core workflows off closed APIs, yet just 1 in 10 has automated monitoring to catch a production model drifting or failing.

title: Anthropic wants to develop its own drugs
blurb: Anthropic said it will develop its own drugs, starting with treatments for "neglected" diseases, alongside the launch of Claude Science, an AI workbench that consolidates scientific tools and datasets into one environment. The move puts Anthropic in the unusual position of selling AI software to pharma customers while competing with them, joining a drug-discovery race that already includes Isomorphic Labs, Insilico, and OpenAI.

title: Trump Administration Lifts Restrictions on Anthropic’s Claude Models After Cybersecurity Alarm
blurb: The Trump administration lifted its ban on Anthropic's Claude models, restoring wide access to Claude Fable 5 and limiting the more powerful Mythos 5 to government-approved US organizations. The Commerce Department had blocked the models on June 12 after Amazon researchers found a way to bypass Fable 5's safeguards to exploit software vulnerabilities, part of a new 30-day federal review framework that also constrained OpenAI's GPT-5.6 Sol.

title: Google DeepMind and A24 announce first-of-its-kind research partnership
blurb: Google DeepMind and A24 announced a research partnership to develop AI tools and workflows alongside the studio's filmmakers, with Google also taking an investment stake in A24. The multi-project collaboration puts DeepMind researchers inside the creative process to test and iterate on new technology, giving the lab direct feedback from working artists while A24 helps shape the tools before they ship.

title: Scattered Spider suspect extradited over $8 million ransom scheme
blurb: Federal prosecutors extradited 19-year-old Peter Stokes, a dual U.S.-Estonian citizen, from Finland to face charges tied to Scattered Spider's breach of a luxury jewelry retailer and an $8 million ransom demand. The DOJ links the group, also tracked as Octo Tempest and UNC3944, to over 100 intrusions and more than $100 million in ransom payments, all built on social-engineering employees rather than exploiting software.

title: Microsoft and Amazon devote billions of dollars to thousands of FDEs
blurb: Microsoft and Amazon are committing billions to embed thousands of engineers directly inside customer teams to deploy agentic AI. Microsoft's $2.5B Frontier Company will place 6,000 experts to co-design AI systems, while AWS put $1B into its own forward-deployed engineer platform, moves that challenge the systems integrators who have long owned enterprise IT rollouts.

title: OpenAI mulling giving US gov't a 5% stake in the company, days after Washington delayed GPT-5.6 — Altman reportedly wants every leading U.S. AI lab paying into an Alaska-style public fund
blurb: OpenAI has discussed handing the U.S. government a 5% stake, worth roughly $42.6 billion at its $852 billion valuation, with Sam Altman proposing that every leading U.S. AI lab contribute the same share into an Alaska Permanent Fund-style vehicle that pays public dividends. The talks are early-stage and would likely require an act of Congress, and rivals Google, Meta, and Anthropic have given no sign they would participate.

title: Postgres transactions are a distributed systems superpower
blurb: The DBOS team argues that workflow state should live inside the same Postgres database as your application data, not in a separate store. Because co-located workflow metadata and application data can be updated in a single transaction, partial failures become impossible, which cleanly solves idempotency and atomicity problems that otherwise require extra bookkeeping tables to guard against duplicate side effects.

title: Launch HN: Manufact (YC S25) – MCP Cloud
blurb: Manufact, a YC S25 startup, launched MCP Cloud, a platform for building and deploying MCP apps and servers to ChatGPT and Claude marketplaces. It pairs the open-source mcp-use SDK with push-to-deploy hosting, cross-client testing against GPT, Claude, and Gemini, and a Cloud Inspector for tracing and replaying MCP traffic in production.

title: Too many tables are bad for you
blurb: Postgres performance degrades as table counts climb into the thousands, straining autovacuum, planning, and catalog lookups.

title: OpenAI proposed donating 5% of its equity to a US sovereign wealth fund
blurb: Sam Altman proposed donating 5% of OpenAI's equity to a US sovereign wealth fund, per an FT report, with other AI companies contributing similar stakes. The move is framed as securing goodwill with the Trump administration and blunting political blowback, though the talks remain preliminary and any formal action would likely require congressional approval.

title: Google launches Nano Banana 2 Lite and Gemini Omni Flash
blurb: Google released Nano Banana 2 Lite and Gemini Omni Flash, adding a faster image model and a video generation model to its Gemini API. Nano Banana 2 Lite generates images in four seconds at $0.034 per 1,000 images, while Gemini Omni Flash produces and conversationally edits up to 10 seconds of video from text, image, and clip inputs at $0.10 per second.

title: Microsoft releases new Windows 11 Start menu, and list of customization policies out too
blurb: Microsoft released the redesigned Windows 11 Start menu, a scrollable single-page layout with a larger pinned area and category views for the all-apps list. Alongside it, the company published a set of group policies that let IT admins control customization options, giving enterprises more say over how the new menu appears on managed devices.

title: Startup targets datacenters with 3D-printed nuclear reactor module
blurb: A startup is pitching datacenter operators a small modular nuclear reactor built largely from 3D-printed components, aiming to cut fabrication cost and build time versus conventional plants. The move reflects growing pressure to secure dedicated, always-on power for AI compute clusters as grid capacity struggles to keep pace with demand.

title: Microsoft unveils $2.5B ‘Frontier Company’ to embed AI engineers inside customers
blurb: Microsoft launched The Microsoft Frontier Company, a $2.5B organization of 6,000 engineers that embeds staff inside customers to build and run AI systems on-site. The move escalates a forward-deployed engineering race pioneered by Palantir, following Amazon's $1B initiative two days earlier, OpenAI's $4B-backed Deployment Company, and Anthropic's $1.5B venture with Goldman Sachs and Blackstone.

title: Intel confirms price hikes on select consumer and server CPUs citing supply costs and demand — select Xeon processors now over $1,000 more expensive
blurb: Intel confirmed price increases on select consumer and server CPUs, citing rising supply-chain costs and strong demand, with some Xeon data center processors now over $1,000 more expensive. The hikes are targeted rather than across the board: enthusiast Arrow Lake chips like the Core Ultra 7 270K Plus rose $30 to $50, while the flagship Core Ultra 9 285K held at $599, suggesting demand-driven pricing on specific SKUs rather than a uniform cost pass-through.

title: ElevenLabs is in talks for a $22B valuation, doubling its price tag five months after its last raise: report
blurb: ElevenLabs is in early talks for a secondary share sale valuing the AI voice company at roughly $22B, per Bloomberg, doubling its $11B Series D mark from February 2026. The tender, expected to close by September, would let employees and early shareholders cash out without the company raising new capital, on reported ARR of $500M reached in the first four months of 2026.

title: VisionAId: An Offline-First Multimodal Android Assistant for People with Visual Impairment, Featuring Personalized Object Retrieval
blurb: VisionAId is an Android app that turns a commodity smartphone into an offline real-time assistant for the 285 million people living with visual impairment. It runs six on-device models through ONNX Runtime for depth, segmentation, face and banknote recognition, and adds a few-shot pipeline that lets users photograph a personal object and later be guided to it with AR markers, spatial audio, and haptics.

title: Is the iPhone birth control? Causal evidence from AT&T's 2007-2011 monopoly [pdf]
blurb: A new NBER working paper uses the 2007-2011 period when AT&T held an exclusive US iPhone contract to estimate the smartphone's causal effect on birth rates. The authors treat variation in AT&T network coverage as a natural experiment and find that early iPhone access measurably reduced fertility, attributing the drop to time displaced by screen use.

title: How To Corrupt An SQLite Database File
blurb: SQLite's official guide documents how a database engine famously resistant to corruption can still be corrupted, from processes overwriting the file with garbage to stale file descriptors bleeding writes into the wrong file. The notable failure mode involves closed-then-reopened descriptors, prompting SQLite 3.8.1 to refuse low-numbered file descriptors after an error message overwrote a Fossil repository via standard error.

title: Hackers Breached DHS Information-Sharing Network
blurb: Hackers breached the Homeland Security Information Network, the DHS platform federal, state, local, and private-sector partners use to share sensitive but unclassified data, exposing servers and a SharePoint collaboration system between late May and early June. The intrusion lands as the US coordinates security for World Cup games, raising concerns that attackers gained visibility into event security planning and interagency response procedures.

title: IQM, Europe’s first public quantum company, admits the future of the tech is uncertain
blurb: IQM, a full-stack quantum computing company from Finland, went public on the Nasdaq via a SPAC merger at a roughly $1.9B valuation, though shares spent most of their debut below the IPO price. The muted reception followed IQM's own prospectus warning that "large-scale commercial traction of quantum computing technology may never occur," even as it grew from eight customers in 2024 to 22 in 2025.

title: Anthropic is discussing a new custom chip with Samsung
blurb: Anthropic is in talks with Samsung to explore developing a custom AI chip, according to The Information, though the company hasn't decided the chip's purpose, power, or server role. The move follows OpenAI's Broadcom-built "Jalapeño" inference processor and would loosen Anthropic's reliance on Nvidia, even as it says chips from Google, Amazon, and Nvidia remain central to its compute strategy.

title: Investors sue Oracle, say it hid how shaky the OpenAI deal was
blurb: Oracle investors filed a class-action lawsuit alleging the company concealed how unstable its $300B OpenAI cloud deal was ahead of a $25B note sale. The suit, which names Larry Ellison and former CEO Safra Catz, claims Oracle touted a backlog that jumped from $97B to $523B without disclosing that OpenAI had missed revenue targets and privately doubted it could pay for the committed compute.

title: Google’s AI buildout drove 37% increase in electricity use in 2025
blurb: Google's electricity consumption jumped 37% in 2025, its largest annual increase ever, as the company's AI data center buildout accelerated faster than the grid can decarbonize. Its data centers alone drew more than 42 million megawatt-hours, rivaling the total electricity use of countries like New Zealand or Denmark, though Google says clean-energy purchases cut operational emissions 2% over the same period.

title: Valve open source the Steam Machine e-ink screen so you can make your own
blurb: Valve open-sourced the "Inkterface," an e-ink display mod for the Steam Machine's front plate, releasing the full build under an MIT license on its GitLab. The kit combines an Adafruit ESP32 Feather, an eInk breakout board, and a 5.83" monochrome panel, and Valve added a printable STL front plate and an assembly video, leaving pre-built versions to third parties like JSAUX.

title: Amazon has deployed enough satellites to launch Leo service later this year
blurb: Amazon said it now has enough satellites in orbit to begin initial Leo internet service later this year, after a Thursday launch pushed its constellation past 390 satellites. The milestone puts Amazon's low-Earth-orbit network into early competition with SpaceX's Starlink, which holds roughly 10,000 satellites and 10 million subscribers after a four-year head start.

title: Tesla Driver Using Autopilot in Texas Crash Is Charged With Manslaughter
blurb: Texas prosecutors charged a Tesla driver with manslaughter after a crash in which the vehicle, running on Autopilot, drove through a driveway and into a house, killing a woman inside. Front-door camera footage captured the Tesla plowing into the home, adding to the legal scrutiny over who bears responsibility when driver-assistance systems are engaged during fatal accidents.

title: Amazon Leo says its latest launch gives it enough satellites to start broadband internet service
blurb: Amazon Leo's overnight launch of 29 satellites brought its constellation to 396 operational satellites, enough to begin commercial high-speed internet service later this year. The network will still trail SpaceX's Starlink, which has more than 10,000 satellites and 12 million subscribers, and Amazon has yet to announce pricing ahead of full first-generation deployment in mid-2029.

title: Mark Zuckerberg tells staff that AI agents haven’t progressed as quickly as he’d hoped
blurb: Mark Zuckerberg told Meta staff at an internal town hall that AI agent development has not accelerated as quickly as executives expected, and that this year's restructuring, including 8,000 layoffs, was not as "clean" as it should have been. He said the payoff from Meta's AI-focused reorganization has not materialized yet but predicted improvements within three to six months, as the company tracks toward $145B in AI infrastructure spending this year.

title: Cloudflare changes AI crawler access rules
blurb: Cloudflare introduced controls that let website owners set separate access policies for AI crawlers across three categories: Search, Agent, and Training. Available on every plan including Free, the change also shifts defaults starting September 15, 2026, when Training and Agent crawlers will be blocked by default on pages that display ads while Search crawlers stay allowed.

title: Cloudflare will filter out web crawlers that serve AI companies
blurb: Cloudflare will start blocking mixed-use web crawlers that scrape sites for AI training and agents while masquerading as search indexers, defaulting new and free accounts to allow search but block AI training and agent access on pages with ads starting September 15, 2026. The company also renamed its Pay Per Crawl feature to Pay Per Use, which pays site owners when their content appears in AI chatbot answers, launching with Ceramic.AI and You.com as partners.

title: Crusoe in Talks to Raise $3 Billion in Round That May Triple Firm’s Value
blurb: Crusoe is in talks to raise about $3B in a funding round that could triple its valuation. The data center startup supplies AI computing power to customers including Meta and Oracle, positioning it as an infrastructure player as demand for training and inference capacity keeps climbing.

title: Finland’s IQM just proved a European deep tech company can list on Nasdaq without moving to the US
blurb: Finnish quantum computing startup IQM began trading on the Nasdaq Global Select Market on July 2 under the ticker "IQMX," reaching the public market through a SPAC merger that values it at roughly $1.8 billion pre-money. IQM stayed headquartered in Espoo rather than reincorporating in the US, and reported €31 million in 2025 revenue with a €67 million order backlog, a rare commercial track record in a sector where most rivals report near-zero sales.

title: Synthetic Contact with AI Reduces Cross-Partisan Animosity
blurb: In five preregistered studies with 3,960 US partisans, researchers found that brief conversations with AI chatbots representing the opposing political party corrected misperceptions and reduced hostility toward the other side. A single ten-minute chat warmed affect and made participants six percentage points more likely to seek a real cross-partisan conversation, though most of the effect faded within a week.

title: An American Privacy Emergency
blurb: A June 4, 2026 Commerce Department directive (DAO 216-26) rolls back confidentiality protection at the Census Bureau and Bureau of Economic Analysis to 1970s-era techniques, abandoning differential privacy and other modern methods. Cynthia Dwork and cosigning researchers argue the order bypassed required administrative procedures and is politically motivated, and warn it will yield weaker privacy, less useful statistics, or both for datasets like the 2030 Census.

title: The fall of the theorem economy
blurb: The author argues that mathematics produces clarity and understanding, not theorems, and that AI's ability to churn out formal proofs threatens the "theorem economy" while leaving the actual work of math untouched. Drawing on his own unpublished results, he shows that conceiving and conjecturing a theorem is the hard, human part, whereas writing the proof is often mechanical, so automating proofs may reshape mathematical incentives without diminishing genuine understanding.

title: A New Look at AI's Impact on Jobs: Firm-Level AI Spending and Workforce Adjustment
blurb: Ramp analyzed firm-level AI spending against hiring data to measure how software budgets track with workforce changes. The report finds that companies increasing AI outlays tend to slow hiring in affected roles rather than cut headcount outright, suggesting adjustment shows up first in unfilled positions and reallocated work rather than in visible layoffs.

title: The website of the future may assemble itself for every visitor
blurb: Adobe demonstrated "agentic sites" that assemble a personalized web page around each visitor's intent in real time, retrieving from a company's existing content rather than inventing pages from scratch. Principal Scientist Carlos Sanchez showed the approach, which Adobe calls "audience of one," reorganizing a coffee-machine site's copy and product selection for a camping-focused visitor and building pages from open-ended queries.

title: Nvidia floats double-dipping datacenter financing scheme
blurb: Nvidia floated a datacenter financing scheme that would let it profit twice from the same buildout, first by selling GPUs and again by taking a stake in the debt or equity funding those purchases. The arrangement deepens the circular financing already binding Nvidia to its largest AI customers, raising questions about how much of the sector's compute demand rests on Nvidia bankrolling its own sales.

title: Private space pilots are flying orbital missions for the US Space Force
blurb: True Anomaly and Rocket Lab completed Victus Haze, a rendezvous mission for the US Space Force in which one satellite located, intercepted, and imaged another in orbit. Rocket Lab launched its Puma spacecraft 16 hours after notice, while True Anomaly's Jackal found the target from 2,000 kilometers away, demonstrating the kind of on-demand orbital inspection the military wants to outsource to private firms.

title: While you’re watching the World Cup, the feds may be watching you
blurb: US cities hosting the World Cup have spent the months before the tournament expanding their surveillance capabilities, from Kansas City to New York. Both the July 4th fireworks on the National Mall and the July 19th World Cup final have been designated National Special Security Events, and privacy advocates warn that the biometric camera networks and counter-drone systems deployed for these events rarely get dismantled afterward.

title: Autonomous drone startup Quantum Systems raises $1.2 billion as investors pile into defense
blurb: Quantum Systems raised $1.2 billion in a Series D round at an $8 billion valuation, co-led by Blackstone, Noteus, Airbus, and Advent. The German startup builds autonomous unmanned systems for land, air, and sea, and says its drones ran over 19,000 missions in Ukraine in 2025, part of a record $17.4 billion flowing into defense tech this year.

title: Meta reportedly plans to rent out its AI compute, sending AI stocks tumbling — 'Meta Compute' would put company in direct competition with AWS
blurb: Meta reportedly plans to launch Meta Compute, a cloud business selling its excess AI capacity and access to models like Muse Spark, per Bloomberg. The move would pit Meta against AWS, Google Cloud, and Azure, but neoclouds took the hit: CoreWeave fell 10.8% and Nebius dropped 12.4%, since Meta has committed roughly $48B to renting their GPUs.

title: Spain Orders Blacklist of Palantir from Public and Private Companies
blurb: Spain has ordered state-controlled companies to blacklist Palantir from future contracts, citing concerns that the US firm could jeopardize national sovereignty and misuse classified security data. The directive from the prime minister's office covers major entities like Telefónica, Indra, and shipbuilder Navantia, and mirrors similar pushback in France and Germany, though Palantir's existing €16.5M defense intelligence contract runs through November.

title: Claude Fable 5 isn’t permanently leaving subscriptions, Anthropic says
blurb: Anthropic said Claude Fable 5 will drop off Pro, Max, Team, and select Enterprise subscription plans after July 7, moving to usage-credit billing amid demand the company calls very high and hard to predict. A Claude Code lead engineer clarified the change is temporary, with Anthropic aiming to restore Fable 5 as a standard subscription feature once it has enough capacity.

title: Google loses final appeal to overturn €4.1 billion EU fine
blurb: The EU's top court dismissed Google's final appeal, upholding a €4.1 billion ($4.7 billion) antitrust fine over Android agreements that forced manufacturers to pre-install Chrome and Google Search. The ruling closes a case that began with the European Commission's 2018 decision, affirming that Google's pre-installation and anti-fragmentation deals illegally strengthened its dominant position, though Google says it already revised the contracts in 2018.

title: Amazon’s Mechanical Turk to stop accepting new customers – and not even AI can save it
blurb: Amazon will stop accepting new customers for Mechanical Turk, the crowdsourced micro-task marketplace it launched in 2005 to route human labor through an API. The platform once powered data labeling for early machine learning, but demand has eroded as AI systems increasingly handle the annotation and classification work that human Turkers used to perform.

title: Travel app Hopper to pay $35M in FTC settlement over ‘unfairly’ charging hidden fees
blurb: Hopper agreed to pay $35M to settle FTC allegations that its travel app hid fees and pre-selected optional charges for services like "VIP Support" and "Price Freeze." The settlement funds consumer redress and bars Hopper from misrepresenting pricing, extending the FTC's push against dark patterns after similar cases involving Match, StubHub, and Fortnite.

title: US government says it got hacked — again
blurb: DHS is investigating a breach of its Homeland Security Information Network, an unclassified platform that federal, state, and local agencies use to coordinate intelligence and emergency response. Hackers reportedly accessed HSIN servers in late May and June, and Senator Mark Warner warned the exposure risks national security, coming after a year of deep cuts to Homeland Security and CISA that raise fresh questions about the government's ability to defend its own systems.

title: FAA proposal: Supersonic airliners can fly over US cities if they’re quiet
blurb: The FAA proposed replacing its 53-year ban on overland supersonic flight with a noise-based standard that caps sonic boom overpressure at the surface below 0.11 pounds per square foot. The threshold reflects Boom Supersonic's demonstrated Mach cutoff flights, though critics note the overpressure metric was discarded by UN experts in 2014 because it doesn't measure perceived loudness or annoyance.

title: Sources: Anthropic's bankers have hired UK law firm Freshfields to advise on its IPO; it also advised on Google's acquisition of Wiz and ServiceNow's Armis deal (The Information)
blurb: Anthropic's bankers have hired UK law firm Freshfields to advise on the company's planned IPO, according to The Information. Freshfields previously worked on Google's acquisition of Wiz and ServiceNow's Armis deal, and its involvement signals that Anthropic is moving into concrete preparation for a public listing.

title: Jeff Bezos' family office backed five AI startups in June
blurb: Bezos Expeditions made five direct AI investments in June, becoming 2026's most active family office with eight private-company deals so far this year, per Fintrx data. The startups span AI approaches: chemistry-focused CuspAI, brain-inspired Flourish, robotics-oriented Generalist, and gameplay-trained General Intuition, alongside a $12B Series B for Prometheus, the $41B invention startup Bezos cofounded and co-leads.

title: To Reduce Electrical Grid Strain Amid Heat Wave, Data Centers Are Ordered to Use Backup Power
blurb: The Trump administration is directing PJM and other grid operators to require data centers to run on backup generators during the current heat wave, freeing grid capacity as triple-digit temperatures spike electricity demand. The order taps backup power that usually sits idle, a stopgap that highlights how AI-driven data center growth is now straining grids fast enough to force emergency load management.

title: Global venture funding hits record $510B in first half as AI boom accelerates
blurb: Global startups raised a record $510B in the first half of 2026, more than all of last year and beating the previous half-year record of $375B, according to Crunchbase. AI drove the surge, with OpenAI and Anthropic alone taking $217B, or 43% of all funding, while AI companies overall captured more than 70% of second-quarter capital.

title: An interview with Sriram Krishnan, who says "there will not be an FDA for AI" under Trump, blames the AI backlash on the industry's "doomer" messaging, and more (Financial Times)
blurb: In an FT interview, White House AI advisor Sriram Krishnan said there will be no "FDA for AI" under Trump, arguing that heavy pre-market regulation would slow US development. He blamed the public backlash on the industry's own "doomer" messaging, contending that existential-risk rhetoric has fueled distrust rather than caution.

title: Your iPhone will soon warn you before you fall for a scam
blurb: Apple introduced Trust Insights, an on-device anti-fraud system in iOS 27 that watches for behavioral signs of scam manipulation during calls, texts, and emails, then adds warnings, delays, or verification steps before a payment or account change goes through. The framework analyzes response timing and usage deviations locally without reading message contents, covers five activity categories at launch, and brings iPhones capability similar to the scam detection already on Google's Pixel phones.

title: True Anomaly Approaches, Images Rocket Lab Craft
blurb: True Anomaly located, approached, and imaged a Rocket Lab spacecraft acting as a non-cooperative target, completing the first VICTUS HAZE mission for Space Systems Command. Rocket Lab launched within 17 hours of notice, True Anomaly found the craft within hours, and it delivered imagery in 61 hours against a 72-hour deadline, demonstrating tactically responsive orbital operations.

title: Malware found spreading through sponsored ad on X
blurb: Jamf Threat Labs found a ClickFix malware campaign running as a sponsored ad on X, promoted through a verified account with a large following and posing as the legitimate Mac app DynamicLake. The ad redirected to a lookalike domain that instructed users to paste Terminal code, installing an Atomic Stealer variant, and passed X's automated ad checks in a repeat of past malicious-ad failures at Google.

title: Cheap Chinese chips could offer way out of RAM price crisis, Apple suggests
blurb: Apple is reportedly in talks to buy memory chips from Chinese makers CXMT and YMTC to blunt a global RAM shortage, despite both sitting on a Pentagon blacklist of firms tied to Beijing's military. The move would require Trump administration sign-off and raises supply-chain security questions for enterprise IT buyers, who would inherit the risk of unvetted chips in Apple hardware.

title: SK hynix to invest $712.5 billion in South Korean operations — Cheongju NAND expansion, Yongin Semiconductor Cluster for DRAM detailed
blurb: SK hynix announced plans to invest KRW 1.1 trillion, roughly $712.5 billion, across its South Korean operations over the coming decades, spanning DRAM, 3D NAND, and HBM packaging. The near-term detail centers on a $64 billion Cheongju expansion for a new M17 NAND fab and HBM back-end capacity, though most of that output only comes online around 2029.

title: Blackstone-owned QTS abandons planned world’s largest data center campus after years of lawsuits — 2,100-acre Virginia Digital Gateway project dies over a newspaper-notice technicality
blurb: QTS, the Blackstone-owned developer behind Virginia's Digital Gateway, terminated plans for what would have been the world's largest data center campus, withdrawing its final appeal after years of lawsuits. The 2,100-acre, gigawatt-scale project near the Manassas Civil War battlefield collapsed after courts voided the county's rezoning over improper public notices, leaving the land under rural zoning.

title: Nvidia recruits longtime Microsoft sales leader Nick Parker with $40M+ pay package
blurb: Nvidia hired 26-year Microsoft sales veteran Nick Parker as executive vice president of worldwide field operations, effective Aug. 24, with a pay package worth over $40M including $35M in restricted stock. Parker, who most recently ran Microsoft's worldwide commercial sales business, will report directly to Jensen Huang and succeed retiring sales chief Jay Puri, inheriting the operation that sells Nvidia's chips to the world's largest companies.

title: Inside the race to power AI data centers with fusion energy — and the surprise detours along the way
blurb: Helion Energy signed an agreement to sell fusion power to Microsoft for a Central Washington data center, targeting 2028 for what it hopes will be the world's first commercial fusion plant. Backed by a $1.5B war chest, Helion is racing ahead while Seattle-area rival Zap Energy, which has raised $330M, hedges by pursuing nuclear fission alongside fusion for near-term revenue.

title: Zoom snaps up Seattle startup Common Room to bolster AI-powered sales tools
blurb: Zoom acquired Seattle startup Common Room, whose AI platform tracks buying signals across sales and marketing teams, for undisclosed terms. Zoom will fold Common Room's buyer intelligence into its Revenue Accelerator platform, pairing account-in-market signals with the sales-call data Zoom already captures so reps know who to reach before a call happens.

title: IQM becomes first European quantum computing company to list on a major US exchange
blurb: IQM Quantum Computers listed on Nasdaq under IQMX, becoming the first European quantum computing company on a major US exchange with a €337M pro forma cash position. The company has sold 23 quantum computers worldwide, more than any other manufacturer, deploying its full-stack superconducting systems to customers including CINECA, the Leibniz Supercomputing Center, and Oak Ridge National Laboratory.

title: Quantum Systems signed a $1.2 billion round at a valuation of about $8 billion — and one of the investors co-leading it is Airbus, exactly the kind of defense prime the startup’s founder says his company could disrupt
blurb: Quantum Systems raised a $1.2B Series D at roughly $8B post-money, more than doubling the Munich drone maker's valuation in a round co-led by Blackstone, Advent, Noteus, and Airbus. Airbus backing the self-described "neo prime" is notable because founder Florian Seibel pitches the company as a threat to exactly the kind of traditional defense prime Airbus represents, and the two agreed to deepen their strategic collaboration alongside the deal.

title: AI can't be listed as inventor on patent applications, Japan's top court rules
blurb: Japan's Supreme Court ruled that an AI system cannot be named as an inventor on a patent application, holding that current law reserves inventorship for natural persons. The decision aligns Japan with similar rulings in the US, UK, and EU, leaving AI-generated inventions in a legal gray zone until legislatures decide whether to rewrite patent statutes.

title: Germany’s Infineon opens major chip plant as EU seeks tech autonomy
blurb: Infineon opened a major new chip plant in Germany, part of the EU's push to reduce reliance on Asian and US semiconductor supply. The facility expands European production of power and automotive chips, a segment where the bloc still holds ground even as it lags on leading-edge logic.

title: No LLM Code in Dependencies
blurb: A git-annex maintainer spent 100 hours purging dependencies containing LLM-generated code, citing incoherent commits and near-copyright-infringement.

title: ChatControl 1.0 mass scanning is to be sneaked through via a 3rd vote next week
blurb: EU lawmakers are set to revive ChatControl's mandatory message-scanning provisions through a third Council vote next week, after prior attempts stalled over privacy objections. The post argues the client-side scanning mandate would break end-to-end encryption for every messaging user in the bloc, and warns that the fresh vote is timed to pass the measure with minimal public scrutiny.

title: PorTAL: Portable Task Adapters for LLMs
blurb: PorTAL introduces portable task adapters that transfer LoRA-style fine-tuning across base models, so adapters survive new model releases.

title: SoftBank enters the rent-a-GPU race as America looks for support for AI training
blurb: SoftBank is entering the GPU rental market, offering AI training capacity from the 10 GW US datacenter buildout it is constructing. The move gives SoftBank a way to monetize its massive server farm as American AI firms scramble for training compute, putting the Japanese conglomerate in direct competition with cloud providers and neoclouds like CoreWeave.

title: Tesla saw a massive sales jump in the second quarter
blurb: Tesla delivered more than 480,000 vehicles in Q2, up over 120,000 from Q1 and its best-ever second quarter by raw deliveries. The result easily beat Wall Street expectations and marks Tesla's strongest quarter since Q3 2025, as geographic expansion and cheaper Model 3, Model Y, and Cybertruck versions help offset a two-year decline in overall sales.

title: Artificial cell manages a few rounds of cell division
blurb: A University of Minnesota lab led by Kate Adamala built a synthetic "SpudCell" that wraps genetic material in a membrane, continually imports fresh raw materials, and spontaneously divides through a few generations before failing. The system spreads roughly 90,000 DNA bases across seven circular molecules and copies them using proteins from the Phi29 bacteriophage, offering a new route to study how membranes enclosed the first cells.

title: Letter: chip sector group SEMI, including Micron and Samsung, warns Scott Bessent US policies affecting prices or production capacity would worsen the shortage (Maggie Eastland/Bloomberg)
blurb: SEMI, the chip industry group whose members include Micron and Samsung, sent a letter warning Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent that US policies raising prices or curbing production capacity would deepen the ongoing semiconductor shortage. The group urged Washington to weigh tariff and trade decisions against supply-chain effects, arguing that measures meant to boost domestic manufacturing could instead tighten an already-strained market.

title: Hong Kong Rises as Crucial Node in Asia’s $2 Trillion AI Trade
blurb: Hong Kong has become a critical hub in a $2 trillion Asian trade network for AI hardware, serving as the main conduit for high-tech products flowing in and out of China. The city's role as a gateway has deepened as the global AI boom drives demand for chips and servers, positioning it at the center of supply chains that route around US export controls.

title: AI Factories Create Winners and Losers in Power Equipment Market
blurb: The buildout of AI factories is reshaping the power equipment market, splitting suppliers into winners and losers as data centers demand transformers, switchgear, and grid gear at unprecedented scale. Firms are reworking their portfolios to capture a market Bloomberg expects to exceed $200B a year, rewarding those positioned for high-density compute loads and squeezing those slow to adapt.

title: Chip Industry Urges US to Avoid Moves That Distort Memory Market
blurb: A semiconductor industry group warned the Trump administration against intervening in memory chip prices or production, arguing that such moves would deepen a historic supply shortage rather than ease it. The group said the squeeze is driven by surging AI demand, and that government attempts to steer capacity or pricing would distort a market already straining to keep up.

title: Trump bought Apple, Nvidia and other tech giants before tariff reversal fueled rebound
blurb: Trump made 327 stock purchases on April 8, 2025, near the market's tariff-driven bottom, concentrating on mega-cap tech names like Apple and Nvidia that his own "liberation day" policy had just battered, according to CNBC's analysis of his 2025 financial disclosure. The next morning he posted "THIS IS A GREAT TIME TO BUY" and began walking back the tariffs, sending the S&P 500 up 9.5% and roughly 50% since.

title: Overland AI lands Marine Corps deal worth nearly $20M to build self-driving military vehicles
blurb: Overland AI won a nearly $20M U.S. Marine Corps contract to build more than a dozen autonomous ground vehicles, becoming the first ground-autonomy company to serve as prime contractor on a military production deal. The Seattle startup, which has raised over $140M and employs more than 100 people, will begin deliveries in early 2027 with vehicles that resupply drone-defense crews across GPS-denied terrain.

title: Artemis II’s Nikon Z9 Was Way More Important for Science Than Expected
blurb: Researchers at Tokyo City University published a paper in The Astrophysical Journal Letters analyzing solar corona images the Artemis II astronauts captured during their lunar flyby with a Nikon Z9. Though the off-the-shelf camera was not photometrically calibrated for the work, the team used background stars to correct it and measure the Sun's F-corona, a diffuse dust-scattered light that is hard to observe from Earth's atmosphere.

title: Deutschlands nächstes Mega-Unicorn: Drohnen-Startup Quantum Systems sammelt 1,2 Milliarden ein
blurb: Quantum Systems, a Munich defense-tech startup building autonomous drones, raised $1.2B at roughly an $8B valuation in one of Europe's largest defense funding rounds, led by Blackstone, Advent, Noteus, and Airbus. The company, which pivoted from agricultural drones to military systems after Russia's invasion of Ukraine, is already profitable and targets around €700M in 2026 revenue while expanding its MOSAIC UXS platform across air, ground, and maritime systems.

title: Fintech unicorn GCash to raise $1.5B in potential Philippines record IPO
blurb: Mynt, the Ayala-backed operator of Philippine e-wallet GCash, is raising 92.3 billion pesos ($1.5B) in an October IPO that would rank as the country's largest ever. The listing values Mynt at $10.9B, above top lender BDO Unibank, and lets backers including Ant Financial and Warburg Pincus sell down after GCash grew to 90 million registered users.

title: SoftBank unveils plans to enter the US neocloud business with SB Neo
blurb: SoftBank plans to launch SB Neo, a US neocloud that rents AI chip access to hyperscalers and other compute-hungry customers, with operations starting in fiscal 2027. SoftBank Corp. will hold 51% and SoftBank Group 49%, building on a GPU cloud beta running in Japan since April, with a target of supplying roughly 10 gigawatts of compute capacity by 2030.

title: Amazon wants to design in-house chips for Kindles, Fire TV, and Echo speakers
blurb: Amazon plans to design its own chips for Kindle, Fire TV, Echo, Alexa devices, Blink cameras, and Ring doorbells, shifting away from third-party processors starting in 2027, according to analyst Ming-Chi Kuo. The move follows a 95% year-over-year drop in free cash flow to roughly $1.2B, with Amazon tapping Alchip as its back-end design partner and targeting around 40 million in-house units annually.

title: Meta has released an app for making generative AI games
blurb: Meta soft-launched Pocket, a mobile app that lets users vibe-code their own minigames from text prompts, available on iOS and Android since June 29. The company has made no public announcement and the app is not yet available in the US, and reporting suggests Pocket grew out of Meta's earlier acqui-hire of the team behind Gizmo, whose "gizmos" naming it reuses.

title: Supermicro denies that its offices were raided by Taiwanese authorities in Nvidia GPU smuggling case — company says that it coordinated with the police and provided access to investigated employees’ workstations and gadgets
blurb: Supermicro denied that Taiwanese authorities raided its Taipei office in an ongoing Nvidia AI GPU smuggling investigation, saying it voluntarily gave police access to the workstations and devices of suspected employees and placed them on administrative leave. The probe is part of Taiwan's effort to curb chip smuggling into China, and three Supermicro-linked individuals, including co-founder Wally Liaw, have already been arrested in the US on export-control charges.

title: Google hit with £3.5bn fine following 8-year battle
blurb: The EU won its eight-year antitrust case against Google, upholding a €4.1B (£3.5bn) fine for using Android to force manufacturers to pre-install its search app and Chrome browser. The dismissed appeal brings Google's total EU antitrust penalties to nearly €11B over the past decade, though the company says it already adapted its agreements to comply back in 2018.

title: EFF letter to FTC on X consent order [pdf]
blurb: EFF and allied privacy groups urged the FTC to reject X Corp.'s petition to set aside a 2022 consent order that fined the company $150M and mandated security reporting through 2042. X argues that new leadership and its pivot to AI justify ending oversight, but the letter counters that FTC orders bind the corporate entity regardless of staff turnover, citing Grok's training on user data and a 2025 breach.

title: Europe is coming for loot boxes, and the games industry is bracing
blurb: A cluster of European regulations is targeting how games sell to minors, with loot boxes as the main focus. PEGI now rates any game with loot boxes as unsuitable for under-16s, and the EU's Digital Fairness Act, expected to pass next year, could ban them outright in games children can access. Because companies tend to change games globally rather than region by region, the rules could dent the $23B chance-based mechanics market worldwide.

title: Finnish quantum computing company IQM closed up 2% in its Nasdaq debut Thursday after going public via a SPAC merger at a ~$1.9B valuation (Anna Heim/TechCrunch)
blurb: Finnish quantum computing company IQM made its Nasdaq debut Thursday, closing up 2% after going public through a SPAC merger at a roughly $1.9B valuation. The listing gives IQM public-market capital to compete against US and Chinese quantum rivals, and marks one of the few European quantum firms to reach the public markets.

title: Starlink Dominates Internet From Space. Can It Disrupt AT&T and Verizon on the Ground?
blurb: SpaceX is pushing Starlink beyond rural broadband toward serving cellphones directly in dense urban markets, taking aim at AT&T and Verizon. The plan faces steep capacity and infrastructure limits, since satellite spectrum cannot yet match the throughput of terrestrial cell towers in crowded cities, leaving Starlink a supplement rather than a replacement for now.

title: Rocket Report: Indian startup nears first launch; SpaceX's millenary milestone
blurb: Katalyst Space Technologies' Link servicing satellite reached orbit to attempt the first commercial reboost of NASA's Swift observatory, which is drifting toward reentry after 22 years without an in-orbit servicing plan. The mission flew on Northrop Grumman's air-launched Pegasus XL, the rocket's final scheduled flight after cheaper options from SpaceX and Rocket Lab pushed the 1990s-era launcher into retirement.

title: Inside the history of DRAM price-fixing lawsuits — how HBM allocations could make a difference after two decades of failed cases
blurb: Seventeen plaintiffs sued Samsung, SK hynix, and Micron in the Northern District of California, alleging the trio, which controls roughly 90% of the DRAM market, coordinated supply cuts that drove memory prices up around 700% in four years. The case is the third antitrust assault on DRAM makers in two decades and leans on HBM allocation data to prove the agreement that sank a nearly identical suit in 2020.

title: Blackstone's QTS abandons plans to build its portion of a 2,100-acre data center campus in Virginia, after years of organized opposition and legal challenges (Dawn Lim/Bloomberg)
blurb: Blackstone's QTS data center unit scrapped its portion of a planned 2,100-acre data center campus in Virginia, ending the project after years of organized community opposition and legal challenges. The reversal shows that local resistance is now a material constraint on hyperscale buildout, even as AI demand pushes operators to secure land and power at record pace.

title: Amazon’s Starlink rival is set to launch satellite internet later this year
blurb: Amazon will begin offering its Leo satellite internet service later this year, after its latest ULA Atlas V launch pushed the constellation to 394 satellites in orbit. The low-Earth-orbit network, formerly Project Kuiper, aims to eventually deploy more than 3,200 satellites and take on Starlink's roughly 10,000, starting service near the poles before expanding toward the equator.

title: Microsoft's alleged unreleased lightweight Edge-based Windows 11 AI OS leaks
blurb: Leaked screenshots and videos reveal an unreleased Microsoft project for a lightweight, Edge-based version of Windows 11 built around AI. The never-shipped OS would have leaned on the Edge browser engine and integrated AI features, suggesting Microsoft explored a stripped-down Windows variant before shelving it.

title: Zuckerberg Says Meta's AI Agents Lag as Wang Claims Model Caught GPT-5.5
blurb: Zuckerberg told Meta staff at a July 2 town hall that the company's AI agent work "hadn't accelerated in the way we expected" over the past four months and that its restructuring bets "haven't come to fruition yet." In the same meeting, AI chief Alexandr Wang claimed Meta's next model caught up with OpenAI's GPT-5.5 on benchmarks, as Meta plans $125-145B in 2026 AI capex and shares closed down 4.9%.

title: Right to Local Intelligence
blurb: A manifesto arguing users should have the right to run AI models locally on their own hardware, free from vendor lock-in.

title: Why Switzerland has 25 gbit internet and America doesn't
blurb: The author argues that Switzerland's 25 Gbit symmetric residential fiber, offered by multiple competing providers at low prices, disproves the assumption that lighter regulation produces better broadband. Switzerland's heavily regulated telecom sector mandates open access to shared infrastructure, while the deregulated US and over-regulated Germany both landed in single-provider markets, showing that the specific rules matter more than the raw amount of regulation.

title: Weave’s $7,999 Isaac 1 bets home robots don’t need legs or fingers
blurb: Weave Robotics launched Isaac 1, a $7,999 home robot that rolls on wheels and grips with two claws instead of walking on legs or using fingers, and handles a narrow chore list like picking up dirty clothes, folding laundry, and making beds. The YC-backed design undercuts humanoid rivals such as 1X's $20,000 Neo by skipping the expensive actuators legs require, though autonomy is partial and deliveries start in California in September.

title: The chip industry has a warning for Trump: hands off the memory market
blurb: SEMI, a semiconductor industry group, warned senior Trump officials that price caps or production mandates aimed at easing the memory shortage would only prolong it, and urged tax breaks and long-term supply deals instead. The crunch stems from AI demand outpacing supply, and with SEMI projecting just 19% annual capacity growth against years-long factory build times, rising prices on phones, laptops, and cars look set to persist.

title: OpenAI offers feds a stake, Anthropic gets out of AI model jail and Meta wants to be a neocloud
blurb: OpenAI reportedly floated giving the U.S. government a 5% stake, the Trump administration lifted export controls on two of Anthropic's most powerful models, and Meta signaled plans to sell cloud compute until its own AI needs it. The roundup also flags Etched launching with $800M for inference chips, South Korea's $584B chip initiative with Samsung and SK hynix, and Michael Burry shorting the AI trade he calls "the beginning of the end.

title: AI Writes Faster Than Humans Can Review: A Longitudinal Study of an Enterprise 2x Mandate
blurb: A longitudinal study tracked a mid-sized company's "2x" mandate to double merged pull requests per engineer, analyzing 802 developers and 196,212 PRs from January 2024 to April 2026. Per-capita throughput hit 2.09x the pre-mandate baseline, with a difference-in-differences design linking most of the gain to AI adoption and accumulated use rather than the mandate itself. Review restructured around automation: per-reviewer load roughly doubled and automated review overtook human review, while merge and revert rates held steady.

title: Critical Cursor AI Code Editor Flaws Could Lead to OS-Level Remote Code Execution
blurb: Cato Networks disclosed two critical vulnerabilities in the Cursor AI code editor, tracked as CVE-2026-50548 and CVE-2026-50549 and dubbed DuneSlide, that could enable remote code execution on the underlying OS outside the IDE's sandbox. Both flaws abuse Cursor's automatic terminal command execution to escape sandbox boundaries via prompt injection, and were patched in Cursor 3.0, released April 2.

title: Is One Layer Enough? A Single Transformer Layer Matches Full-Parameter RL Train
blurb: This paper challenges the assumption that RL post-training benefits every transformer layer equally, showing that training a single layer can recover most of the gains from full-parameter RL and sometimes exceed them. Across seven Qwen3 and Qwen2.5 models, three RL algorithms, and tasks spanning math, code, and agentic decision-making, the high-contribution layers consistently cluster in the middle of the stack, with rankings that stay stable across datasets and methods.

title: CursorBench 3.1
blurb: Cursor released CursorBench 3.1, an evaluation suite that scores coding agents on ambiguous, multi-file tasks drawn from real Cursor sessions. The update adds problems focused on codebase understanding, bugfinding, planning, and code review, and reports average cost per task by applying each model's published token pricing to the tokens it used.

title: Smooth AI criminal drives 'first' end-to-end agentic ransomware attack
blurb: Researchers documented what they describe as the first end-to-end agentic ransomware attack, in which an AI system autonomously handled reconnaissance, intrusion, and payload deployment with minimal human direction. The case signals a shift from AI as a tactical assist to AI running the full attack chain, raising the pressure on defenders to counter automated adversaries operating at machine speed.

title: Program-as-Weights: A Programming Paradigm for Fuzzy Functions
blurb: This paper introduces fuzzy-function programming, which compiles a natural-language spec into a compact, locally-executable neural artifact instead of routing each call to an LLM API. Their Program-as-Weights system uses a 4B compiler trained on the released 10M-example FuzzyBench dataset to emit adapters for a frozen 0.6B Qwen3 interpreter, matching direct Qwen3-32B prompting while using about one-fiftieth the inference memory and running at 30 tokens/s on a MacBook M3.

title: Controllable Sim Agents with Behavior Latents
blurb: CNeVA (Controllable Neural Variational Agents) is a traffic-simulation framework that infers a per-agent Gaussian behavior latent from per-channel discounted returns, conditioning a rectified-flow trajectory generator so engineers can steer agents along interpretable axes like speed, acceleration, and safety. On the Waymo Open Motion Dataset it reaches competitive realism while exposing per-channel controllability that higher-ranked imitation models lack, using soft eligibility gates to preserve gradient signal near reward thresholds.

title: WattGPU: Predicting Inference Power and Latency on Unseen GPUs and LLMs
blurb: WattGPU is a pair of predictive models that estimate mean GPU power draw and inter-token latency for LLM inference using only public model metadata and GPU specifications, with no profiling or hardware access required. Across 42 open-source LLMs and 8 GPUs, it achieves median absolute percentage errors as low as 3.4% on unseen GPUs and cuts error roughly 4x versus TDP and roofline baselines.

title: Startup activates nuclear microreactor live on stage to power an Nvidia RTX Spark desktop PC — firm working with Nvidia to build a 30MW closed loop AI factory that doesn’t use local water
blurb: Valar Atomics activated its Ward 250 nuclear microreactor live on stage and used it to power an Nvidia RTX desktop running a Blackwell chip, alongside a partnership with Nvidia to build a 30MW closed-loop AI factory. The reactor ran at 37% power to serve a live website, and the closed-loop cooling design consumes no local water, targeting the power and water bottlenecks facing AI data centers.

title: Coding Agents Are Guessing: Measuring Action-Boundary Violations in Underspecified DevOps Instructions
blurb: UnderSpecBench is a benchmark that measures action-boundary violations in LLM coding agents like Claude Code, Codex, and OpenCode on 2,208 DevOps prompt variants derived from documented incidents and CVEs. Across five agent-model configurations, 55.8% to 67.8% of runs violated at least one safety boundary, showing that underspecified instructions push agents to guess rather than fail, and that completion-centric benchmarks overstate safe autonomy.

title: Crunchbase Data: Global Startup Investment Hit Record $510B In H1 2026 As AI Boom Accelerates Funding And Exits
