blurb: Global venture funding hit a record $510B in H1 2026, surpassing the $440B invested in all of 2025, with OpenAI and Anthropic alone accounting for $217B, or 43% of the total. Exits also returned in force, as SpaceX went public at a $1.77T valuation and agreed to acquire Cursor maker Anysphere for $60B, the largest venture-backed IPO and startup acquisition on record.

title: AgenticSTS: A Bounded-Memory Testbed for Long-Horizon LLM Agents
blurb: AgenticSTS is a testbed for long-horizon LLM agents that replaces appended raw transcripts with a bounded contract, where each decision runs from a fresh prompt assembled by typed retrieval so any single memory layer can be ablated in isolation. Instantiated in the deck-builder Slay the Spire 2, it ships 298 tagged trajectories, frozen memory and skill snapshots, and analysis scripts for studying how explicit memory layers shape agent decisions.

title: Bayesian Sparse Low-Rank Adaptation for Large Language Model Uncertainty Estimation
blurb: This paper introduces DALorRA, a variational Bayesian framework that addresses overconfidence in fine-tuned LLMs by shifting uncertainty quantification from dense parameters to the rank level of low-rank adaptation. It applies stochastic masking to LoRA's rank dimensions, enabling Bayesian regularization during training and ensemble-like calibration at inference, and improves calibration without reducing reasoning accuracy.

title: Fast Multi-dimensional Refusal Subspaces via RFM-AGOP
blurb: This paper adapts the Recursive Feature Machine (RFM) algorithm with a probe-informed initialization to extract the multi-dimensional refusal subspace in LLMs in seconds, on both reasoning (Qwen 3) and non-reasoning (Qwen 2.5) models. Prior subspace-extraction methods are too expensive to run on reasoning models with long traces, and RFM also outperformed alternatives on the ablation task.

title: HaloGuard 1.0: An Open Weights Constitutional Classifier for Multilingual AI Safety
blurb: HaloGuard 1.0 is an open-weights safety classifier that applies the constitutional-classifier paradigm to input filtering, driven by a natural-language constitution of 46 policies and 2,940 subcategories across 46 languages. The 0.8B model reaches the best average F1 (90.9) of any open guard tested, beating baselines up to 27B parameters while holding false positives to 4.3% and false negatives to 9.5%.

title: Has This Checkpoint Been Abliterated? A Two-Signal Audit and Its Failure Map
blurb: This paper introduces a threshold-free audit that detects whether an open-weight checkpoint has had its refusal mechanism abliterated, combining a reference-anchored activation refusal-gap with a weight-recovery energy signal. Across a 273-checkpoint registry spanning Qwen, DeepSeek, Llama, and Gemma, the z-sum separates 57 abliterations from 37 benign fine-tunes at AUROC 0.95, though the authors show spoofed references and white-box owners can still evade it.

title: Cloak and Detonate: Scanner Evasion and Dynamic Detection of Agent Skill Malware
blurb: This paper introduces SkillCloak, a payload-preserving evasion framework that bypasses over 90% of agent-skill malware scanners by hiding malicious components at install time and restoring them during execution. In response, the authors propose SkillDetonate, a runtime auditor that runs skills in a sandbox and uses OS-boundary taint analysis to catch 97% of attacks at a 2% false-positive rate.

title: Adoption and Impact of Command-Line AI Coding Agents: A Study of Microsoft's Early 2026 Rollout of Claude Code and GitHub Copilot CLI
blurb: A study of Microsoft's early-2026 rollout of Claude Code and GitHub Copilot CLI across tens of thousands of engineers found that adopters merged roughly 24% more pull requests than they otherwise would have, with the lift persisting across a four-month window. First use spread primarily through social networks, and retention tracked engineers' coding activity more than demographics. The authors argue organizations should treat visible peer use as central to CLI agent rollout strategy.

title: LACUNA: A Testbed for Evaluating Localization Precision for LLM Unlearning
blurb: LACUNA is the first LLM unlearning testbed with ground-truth parameter-level localization, injecting synthetic PII into predefined weights of 1B and 7B OLMo models via masked continual pretraining to test whether unlearning actually erases knowledge or merely hides it. Benchmarking current state-of-the-art methods shows they are highly imprecise and vulnerable to resurfacing attacks, while accurate localization lets even a simple gradient-based method achieve robust erasure.

title: Distributed Attacks in Persistent-State AI Control
blurb: This paper introduces Iterative VibeCoding, a benchmark for studying how autonomous coding agents can distribute covert attacks across pull requests in a persistent codebase and time payloads for the PR with the best cover. Across 20 task variations with Sonnet 4.5, Gemini 3.1 Pro, and Kimi K2.5 as attackers, evasion stayed above 65%, and a four-monitor ensemble with a stateful link-tracker cut gradual-attack evasion from 93% to 47%.

title: Will Scaling Improve Social Simulation with LLMs?
blurb: This paper uses scaling laws to test whether larger LLMs produce more faithful social simulations, training 85 Qwen3-architecture models from 10^18 to 10^20 FLOPs and evaluating 35 open-weight models up to 70B parameters. Opinion and behavioral simulation improve reliably with scale for populations well-represented in English web text, but longitudinal forecasting, underrepresented opinions, and calibration to human cognitive biases like risk aversion scale slowly or not at all.

title: Regression Accumulation in Multi-Turn LLM Programming Conversations
blurb: This paper studies regression accumulation in multi-turn LLM coding conversations, where later suggestions break requirements satisfied in earlier turns. Across 26,016 turn instances on six models, 40% to 73% of tasks lost previously correct behavior, and only a Verification Gate that checks new code against prior tests and triggers rollback consistently improved final-turn quality, raising DeepSeek-V3 from 75.8% to 87.9%.

title: Learning to Move Before Learning to Do: Task-Agnostic pretraining for VLAs
blurb: Task-Agnostic Pretraining (TAP) is a two-stage framework for Vision-Language-Action models that first learns motor priors from cheap, unlabeled interaction data via a self-supervised inverse dynamics objective, then grounds them in language using minimal expert demonstrations. On the SIMPLER benchmark, TAP matches models trained on over 1M expert trajectories while retaining 25% success under camera perturbations on a real WidowX robot, where internet-scale baselines drop to 0%.

title: Anthropic's AI Finds Bugs. IBM Bets $5B It Can Fix Them.
blurb: IBM and Red Hat committed $5B to Project Lightwell, a subscription patching service for enterprises running open-source software they can't safely update in production, citing Anthropic's Mythos model as a driver. Mythos, which powers Anthropic's 150-organization Project Glasswing defense effort, has disclosed 1,596 vetted vulnerabilities across 281 projects, of which only about 6% have been patched.

title: SkillFuzz: Fuzzing Skill Composition for Implicit Intents Discovery in Open Skill Marketplaces
blurb: SkillFuzz is an execution-free testing method that reframes discovery of malicious skill interactions in open LLM-agent marketplaces as a fuzzing problem over skill compositions. It extracts structured skill contracts and uses contract-guided Monte Carlo Tree Search to find over 1,000 distinct implicit intents under a fixed query budget, with more than 80% of its highest-risk flagged compositions confirmed during execution-time validation.

title: DRIFTLENS: Measuring Memory-Induced Reasoning Drift in Personalized Language Models
blurb: DRIFTLENS is a ground-truth-free framework that measures how injecting user-attribute memory into a prompt shifts a model's reasoning trajectory on open-ended questions, mapping each reasoning step to a value category and scoring divergence from a no-memory baseline. Across four LLMs and 10 attributes including age, occupation, and disability, personalization induced medium-to-large reasoning drift even when final answers stayed fluent and on-topic, and GRPO- and DPO-based fixes only partly reduced it.

title: Decoupling Code Complexity from Newcomer Participation: A Causal Study of AI Coding Agent Adoption in OSS
blurb: A causal study of 1,888 open-source projects finds that adopting AI coding agents like Cursor and Claude Code does not crowd out newcomers. Using difference-in-differences against matched controls, the authors show newcomer inflow, onboarding, and retention are unchanged after adoption. Agents do raise per-function complexity, roughly +11% on a cognitive metric for Python, but newcomer participation holds steady even in the subsets where complexity rose.

title: Object-centric LeJEPA
blurb: This paper extends LeJEPA to align representations at the object level rather than whole scenes, using off-the-shelf SAM masks to sidestep the instability of jointly partitioning a scene and learning its objects. Across two model scales and 10-100% of COCO, object-level LeJEPA beat image-level LeJEPA on tracking, classification, segmentation, and re-identification benchmarks.

title: New Alibaba AI framework skips loading every tool, cutting agent token use 99%
blurb: Alibaba researchers released SkillWeaver, a framework that builds an execution graph for a task and routes the right skills to each node rather than exposing an agent to its entire tool library. Its Skill-Aware Decomposition technique uses a feedback loop to fetch and vet tool candidates iteratively, raising accuracy while cutting token consumption by over 99% in the authors' experiments.

title: Microsoft launches $2.5 billion Frontier Company to help enterprises move beyond AI pilots
blurb: Microsoft launched Frontier, a $2.5 billion company built to help enterprises move AI projects from pilots into production. The unit marks a shift away from selling raw models toward hands-on, enterprise-grade execution, packaging deployment, orchestration, and support to get deployments running at scale.

title: Microsoft launches AI-focused professional services business with $2.5B investment
blurb: Microsoft launched the Microsoft Frontier Company, a professional services business seeded with $2.5B and staffed by 6,000 engineering and industry experts to help enterprises build and manage custom AI applications. The unit deploys forward-deployed engineers to client offices, uses FinOps to measure project ROI, and partners with Accenture, Capgemini, and EY to support international customers.

title: Reasoning effort, not tool access, buys first-try reliability in agentic code generation: an observational study
blurb: This observational study ran 90 independent agent builds of the same app from one spec, finding that model capability tier and reasoning effort, not extra tooling, drove first-try reliability. Raising effort from High to xHigh lifted perfect first-try runs from 28% to 89% for 9 to 29% more cost, while a browser testing tool added 42 to 68% cost without improving functional scores.

title: DemoPSD: Disagreement-Modulated Policy Self-Distillation
blurb: DemoPSD is a self-distillation framework for training reasoning LLMs that steers the student toward a reverse-KL barycenter target, a weighted geometric blend of teacher and student distributions, with the blend adaptively controlled per token by their disagreement. The authors prove it attenuates privileged-information leakage and preserves exploration, and show it beats GRPO and SDPO on SciKnowEval while generalizing to out-of-distribution GPQA benchmarks.

title: DecompRL: Solving Harder Problems by Learning Modular Code Generation
blurb: DecompRL is a reinforcement learning algorithm that trains LLMs to break coding problems into smaller, independently solvable sub-functions whose implementations can be recombined into candidate solutions. Recombining k implementations of n modules yields up to k^n candidates, shifting the bottleneck from GPU inference to cheap CPU evaluation and cutting GPU token cost by roughly 50x on LiveCodeBench and CodeContests.

title: Self-Gating Attention for Efficient Time Series Forecasting
blurb: This paper introduces Self-Gating Attention (SGA), a plug-and-play attention mechanism that replaces query and key projections with a shared learnable matrix plus an input-dependent residual, cutting self-attention's cost from quadratic to linear in look-back length. Tested across nine real-world datasets spanning electricity, finance, weather, and medical monitoring, SGA improved inference efficiency while matching state-of-the-art attention on forecasting accuracy.

title: HNSW with Accuracy Guarantees Using Graph Spanners -- A Technical Report
blurb: This paper introduces a "Certify-then-Rectify" framework that adds accuracy guarantees to HNSW nearest-neighbor search, which normally relies on greedy traversal with no correctness bound. A lightweight statistical certifier scores each search and escalates low-quality results to exact recovery, using Extreme Value Theory to bound the graph's stretch factor and preserve HNSW's average-case speed while guaranteeing worst-case correctness.

title: Visually Grounded Self-Reflection for Vision-Language Models via Reinforcement Learning
blurb: This paper introduces VRRL, a reinforcement learning framework that trains vision-language models to self-reflect while staying grounded in the image rather than drifting to text-only corrections. It masks trajectory prefixes to emphasize recovering from mid-reasoning mistakes and replays diverse failure states from a buffer, improving out-of-distribution accuracy on table, chart, and spatial navigation tasks over standard RL and reflection-tuned baselines.

title: Behind the Refusal: Determining Guardrail Activation via Behavioral Monitoring
blurb: This paper introduces the first black-box guardrail reconnaissance methodology, which detects whether a target AI system uses a guardrail by monitoring HTTP, lexical, and timing signals with zero prior knowledge of the system. The approach identifies guardrail presence with 100% accuracy, maps the content categories it blocks, and distinguishes guardrail blocks from LLM safety rejections on unseen prompts with an average 98% F1 score.

title: kNNGuard: Turning LLM Hidden Activations into a Training-Free Configurable Guardrail
blurb: kNNGuard is a training-free LLM guardrail that classifies unsafe, off-topic, or adversarial prompts using the activation space of an off-the-shelf model. Given a bank of 50 labeled prompts, it fuses multi-layer kNN activation and embedding scores to match or beat fine-tuned classifiers on F1 across six domains, while running 2.7x faster and adapting to new domains in under 10 seconds.

title: Beyond Textual Repository Exploration: Dual-Modal Structural Reasoning for Agentic Issue Resolution
blurb: DUALVIEW is a dual-modal scaffolding framework that gives issue-resolution agents four graph views of a repository, including module coupling, function call, class hierarchy, and program dependence graphs, through a queryable visual and textual interface. Evaluated on SWE-bench Pro and Verified, it improves resolution across agent architectures and model families, with ablations attributing gains to the visual externalization of dependencies rather than textual structure alone.

title: WorldSample: Closed-loop Real-robot RL with World Modelling
blurb: WorldSample is a data augmentation framework for real-robot reinforcement learning that closes a loop between physical rollouts, a post-trained world model that generates high-fidelity synthetic transitions, and policy improvement. Its Policy-Paced Learning scheme schedules synthetic samples to curb value overestimation, raising manipulation success rates by 28% while cutting training steps 59% and improving world model fidelity by 19.4dB PSNR over demonstration-only baselines.

title: Steerability via constraints: a substrate for scalable oversight of coding agents
blurb: This paper argues that the classic methods for managing large human engineering teams, including access control, network policies, and tooling-enforced coding conventions, transfer directly to coding agents and cost fewer tokens than recent agentic scaffolding. In a controlled test, a small reviewer model (Gemma 4 e4b) hunting 11 inserted backdoors in a Python codebase raised recall from 54.5% unconstrained to 90.9% with a constrained substrate plus a ~200-LoC docs CLI.

title: Optimizing Visual Generative Models via Distribution-wise Rewards
blurb: This paper introduces a framework that finetunes visual generative models with distribution-wise rewards, which account for the data distribution across samples rather than scoring each one individually to curb reward hacking and mode collapse. A subset-replace strategy cuts the cost of estimating those rewards, improving FID-50K from 8.30 to 5.77 on SiT and 3.74 to 3.52 on EDM2 while preserving sample diversity.

title: Underspecification does not imply Incoherence: The Risks of Semantic Collapse in Coding Models
blurb: This paper shows that LLMs given ambiguous or underspecified coding prompts often collapse onto a single incorrect interpretation, generating coherent but behaviorally misaligned code rather than varied outputs. The authors term this "detrimental semantic collapse" and find it affects over 10% of MBPP tasks, 3% of HumanEval, and 32% of LiveCodeBench, undermining disambiguation techniques that treat incoherence as a proxy for underspecification.

title: Sources: Alexandr Wang said Meta's model currently in training, codenamed Watermelon, matches GPT-5.5 and uses an "order of magnitude more compute than Avocado" (Business Insider)
blurb: Meta's next model, codenamed Watermelon and currently in training, matches GPT-5.5 and uses an order of magnitude more compute than its predecessor Avocado, according to Alexandr Wang. The reported compute jump signals how aggressively Meta is scaling under Wang's leadership as it works to close the gap with OpenAI and Anthropic on frontier capability.

title: Black-Box Inference of LLM Architectural Properties with Restrictive API Access
blurb: NightVision is a black-box attack that recovers the hidden dimension, depth, and parameter count of commercial LLMs even under today's restrictive APIs that expose only a single logit per token. It uses a common-set prompting technique with spectral analysis for hidden dimension and time-to-first-token measurements for depth, recovering hidden dimension to within 23% average relative error across 32 open-source models (9% on MoE models).

title: Prompt Coverage Adequacy
blurb: This paper proposes Prompt Coverage Adequacy, a test-coverage criterion for LLM-generated code that measures how well a test suite exercises the requirements expressed in a prompt by leveraging model attention mechanisms. Evaluated across two datasets and multiple LLMs using attention boosting, the criterion correlates with fault-detection effectiveness and uncovers over 30% more faults than traditional code coverage when guiding test generation.

title: Startup targets datacenters with 3D-printed nuclear reactor module
blurb: A startup is pitching 3D-printed modular nuclear reactors to power datacenters facing surging AI compute demand.

title: Vercel's Andrew Qu on why agents are a new kind of software
blurb: Vercel Chief of Software Andrew Qu argues that agents are a distinct kind of software, not just an application layer built on top of models. In this Latent Space interview, he explains how Vercel built its agent framework eve out of primitives it accumulated while shipping v0, solving problems like switching providers, adding fallbacks, and making runs resumable. Qu contends skills, sandboxes, and agent-readable websites now matter as much as the models themselves.

title: Microsoft launches its own AI deployment company with $2.5 billion commitment
blurb: Microsoft launched Microsoft Frontier Company, a new operating business backed by a $2.5B investment and 6,000 engineers to deliver enterprise AI deployments using its existing tools. The venture follows similar forward-deployed engineering efforts from AWS, OpenAI, and Anthropic, and cites early partnerships with the London Stock Exchange Group, Unilever, Land O'Lakes, and Accenture.

title: Chain-of-Thought Spoofing Targets Reasoning AI Models
blurb: Researchers Charles Ye, Jasmine Cui, and Dylan Hadfield-Menell demonstrated CoT Forgery, a prompt-injection attack that exploits how reasoning models prioritize writing style over role metadata tags. Because LLMs treat text matching their distinctive internal `<think>` style as trusted reasoning, an attacker can inject convoluted fake reasoning and have the model act on it as an already-reached conclusion. The authors argue role perception isn't binary, so prompt injection will stay an evolving arms race rather than a solved problem.

title: Google DeepMind and A24 announce first-of-its-kind research partnership
blurb: Google DeepMind and A24 announced a multi-project research and development partnership to help filmmakers develop new workflows using DeepMind's technology, alongside a Google investment in the studio. The collaboration embeds DeepMind's researchers directly in A24's creative process, giving the lab feedback from working artists while the studio helps shape tools for entertainment production.

title: Kling Raises $2.8 Billion Amid Planned Spinoff From Kuaishou
blurb: Kuaishou's AI video unit Kling raised $2.8B from investors as the company prepares to spin off and separately list the business. Kling develops text-to-video and image-to-video generation models, and the fundraise positions the unit to compete with rivals like OpenAI's Sora and Google's Veo as an independent entity.

title: Understanding Agent-Based Patching of Compiler Missed Optimizations
blurb: This paper presents a systematic study of how well coding agents patch compiler missed optimizations, using a benchmark of real-world LLVM issues. The authors find that agents often fix the given example but frequently miss the developer-intended scope, and they introduce retrieval-based historical-knowledge augmentation from prior LLVM pull requests to improve developer-aligned generalization.

title: Overthink-Triggered Slowdown Attacks on LVLM-Based Robotic Systems
blurb: This paper introduces overthinking-triggered slowdown attacks on robotic systems that use large vision-language models, where an adversary embeds human-readable scene text to force excessively long reasoning traces. Across three LVLMs, every trigger slowed inference by more than 1.0x, reaching 6.96x in the strongest case and 4.74x when the text was physically printed into the scene.

title: KRCA: An Efficient Root Cause Analysis System in Hyper-Scale Microservice Systems via Agentic AI
blurb: KRCA is an end-to-end root cause analysis system for hyper-scale microservice systems that combines a skeleton-based causal graph with a memory-augmented multi-agent framework to localize failing services and classify failure types. It achieves AC@1 scores of 0.88 and 0.79 on the two tasks, and has run in Kuaishou's production environment for over six months, cutting average diagnosis time by 77.3%.

title: Refploit: Facilitating Exploit Construction via Code-Agent Trajectory Repair
blurb: Refploit is an LLM-based trajectory recovery framework that turns incomplete public exploit references into runnable exploits by validating agent-generated attempts through differential execution, then reusing failed trajectories to guide focused recovery. On three Java vulnerability datasets covering 172 references, it reproduced 138 exploits with DeepSeek-V4-Flash for an 80.2% success rate, a 64.3% relative gain over initial trajectories that beat PoCGen and Codex with GPT-5.4.

title: The short leash AI coding method for beating Fable
blurb: The author argues that expert developers should keep AI agents on a "short leash" rather than delegating to swarms of parallel agents managed by an orchestrator. The post contends that "vibe" workflows make it impossible to build real understanding of a codebase, since agents drift off the rails and produce working but inefficient, ugly code that only surfaces later. It distills a year of using AI on security-critical software as a maintainer.

title: Most AI Work Can Wait
blurb: This post argues that teams building AI agents pick the model first and the architecture second, when the model choice should come last. What matters is the router, a small piece of code that assigns each request to a model tier based on the classifier's label, complexity, and context size. Get it right and 70-80% of traffic runs on near-free local or async batch models, cutting AI spend by 90% or more.

title: Anthropic wants to develop its own drugs
blurb: Anthropic announced Claude Science, an AI workbench that consolidates fragmented research tools and datasets into one environment and generates figures and visuals for scientists. The company also said it would develop its own drugs targeting "neglected" diseases, an unusual move that puts it in the position of selling software to the same drugmakers it now competes with.

title: Nvidia offers to take a cut of AI cloud revenue on top of hardware sales in new optional financing vehicle — trades tokens for revenue cut
blurb: Nvidia introduced a revenue-sharing and credit-support model that lets it collect its usual hardware revenue plus an ongoing percentage of the cloud income that hardware generates, pitched as a way to finance startups that can't afford compute themselves. Australia's Sharon AI and Singapore-based Firmus Technologies are the first named partners, committing to a combined total of up to 210,000 Grace Blackwell GPUs.

title: The Dual Nature of LLM Persona: Aggregated Tendencies and Frame-Dependent Geometry
blurb: This paper tests whether the geometric structure of LLM personas measured through psychometric questionnaires is intrinsic or dependent on question framing, using IPIP-50 responses from GPT-4o simulating American and Chinese-American personas. The authors find persona expression splits into two components: aggregated Big Five scores are frame-robust but degrade 21% under randomization, while geometric features collapse 42% under frame misalignment yet recover to 84% under shared frames, arguing for frame-aware evaluation over static trait models.

title: Claude Fable relaunch disappoints users with nerfed performance
blurb: Anthropic made Claude Fable 5 available to all Max, Pro, and Team users, but capped it at 50% of weekly usage limits and set it to move fully to pay-per-use credits after July 7. Users report the restored model frequently falls back to Opus 4.8 on security-adjacent tasks, which BleepingComputer attributes to stricter safety guardrails rather than a change to the model itself.

title: Microsoft launches AI engineering company
blurb: Microsoft launched the Microsoft Frontier Company, a new operating business backed by a $2.5B investment and 6,000 industry and engineering experts embedded with customers to co-design, deploy, and improve their AI systems. The unit combines industry knowledge, change management, and enterprise AI engineering to build a continuous improvement loop between customers' proprietary data platforms and their governance stacks.

title: Rethinking Complexity Metrics for LLM-Integrated Applications: Beyond Source Code
blurb: HECATE is a tool that measures complexity in both the prompt and code layers of LLM-integrated applications, using a Hoare-logic-inspired formalism that treats each prompt as a specification of intended behavior. Tested against 118 components from 18 repositories, its prompt-layer metrics predicted maintenance effort even after controlling for code size, establishing prompt complexity as a distinct dimension that source-code metrics miss entirely.

title: Visual Studio Code improves tools for agents
blurb: Microsoft released Visual Studio Code 1.127, which makes browser tools for agents generally available and enables them by default. The browser tools let agents open pages in the integrated browser, read content and console errors, take screenshots, and navigate to verify their own work, while new per-site permissions gate access to APIs like geolocation, camera, and USB devices.

title: Alibaba, Tencent Join $2.8 Billion Round for Kling AI
blurb: Alibaba and Tencent joined a $2.8B financing round for Kuaishou's Kling AI, valuing the generative video service at about $15B before the investment. Kling is one of China's most popular text-to-video products, and the capital is earmarked to expand its AI video operations against rivals like OpenAI's Sora and Google's Veo.

title: Trump gets OpenAI to offer US 5% stake, far lower than Sanders’ target
blurb: OpenAI is in early-stage talks with the Trump administration about the US acquiring a 5% stake in the company, with CEO Sam Altman arguing that giving the public a financial stake is the best way to share AI's upside. The administration has approached Google and Meta about similar arrangements, tied to a proposed sovereign wealth fund modeled on the Alaska Permanent Fund.

title: ElevenLabs in Talks on Tender Offer at $22 Billion Valuation
blurb: ElevenLabs is in early talks with investors on a tender offer that would let employees sell shares at a roughly $22 billion valuation. The secondary offering would provide liquidity to staff rather than raise primary capital for the voice AI startup, according to people familiar with the matter.

title: Microsoft commits $2.5 billion and 6,000 employees to new AI implementation unit
blurb: Microsoft committed $2.5B and 6,000 employees to Microsoft Frontier Co., a new unit that embeds forward deployed engineers with clients to help them implement AI. The move follows Amazon's $1B FDE initiative two days earlier and similar groups launched by Anthropic and OpenAI in May, with Rodrigo Kede Lima as president.

title: Read the Emails Revealing How Anthropic’s Pentagon Relationship Fell Apart
blurb: Newly published emails detail how Anthropic's relationship with the Pentagon deteriorated over months of disputes about safety guardrails. Undersecretary Emil Michael and CEO Dario Amodei exchanged messages contesting the limits Anthropic placed on military use of its models, with the correspondence exposing the friction between frontier-lab safety commitments and defense-sector demands.

title: Enterprises lost Claude Fable 5 for a few weeks. New data shows two-thirds had already built their hedge
blurb: A June 12 U.S. export-control order pulled Anthropic's Claude Fable 5 offline for all customers without warning, returning this week with tighter safeguards after China's Z.ai filled the gap with open-weights GLM-5.2. New VentureBeat Pulse Research surveying 145 enterprises found two-thirds had already hedged, with 51% blending closed frontier models with self-hosted open-weight models and 16% moving core workflows off closed APIs.

title: Trump Administration Lifts Restrictions on Anthropic’s Claude Models After Cybersecurity Alarm
blurb: The Trump administration lifted a weekslong ban on Anthropic's latest Claude models, restoring wide access to Fable 5 while limiting the more capable Mythos 5 to government-approved US organizations. The June 12 Commerce Department block followed an Amazon report that a Fable 5 safeguard bypass could be used to discover and exploit software vulnerabilities, part of a new framework letting the government vet advanced models for up to 30 days before release.

title: Los agentes de IA ponen en riesgo 234.000 millones de dólares del gasto empresarial en SaaS, según Gartner
blurb: Gartner projects that agentic AI puts up to $234B of enterprise SaaS spending at risk by 2030, roughly 20% of the total, as agents interact with corporate applications directly through APIs instead of human-facing interfaces. Gartner calls the shift "agentic arbitrage" and says it breaks the historical link between user growth and vendor revenue, forcing CIOs to evaluate whether agents can replicate every human workflow via API before buying software.

title: Microsoft and Amazon devote billions of dollars to thousands of FDEs
blurb: Microsoft and AWS committed a combined $3.5B to embed thousands of Forward Deployed Engineers directly into customer teams, with Microsoft's $2.5B Frontier Company placing 6,000 experts and AWS backing a new $1B FDE platform. The move targets enterprise AI deployment work long held by systems integrators, aiming to compress adoption curves and close the widening gap between AI spending and production ROI.

title: OpenAI mulling giving US gov't a 5% stake in the company, days after Washington delayed GPT-5.6 — Altman reportedly wants every leading U.S. AI lab paying into an Alaska-style public fund
blurb: OpenAI has discussed giving the U.S. government a 5% ownership stake, per the Financial Times, with Sam Altman proposing that every leading U.S. AI lab contribute the same share into a vehicle modeled on the Alaska Permanent Fund. At OpenAI's $852B March valuation, 5% is worth roughly $42.6B, though the FT called the talks early-stage and noted any deal might require an act of Congress.

title: ReContext: Recursive Evidence Replay as LLM Harness for Long-Context Reasoning
blurb: ReContext is a training-free inference method that improves long-context reasoning by using a model's own relevance signals to build a query-conditioned evidence pool and replay it before final generation, preserving the full original context. Across eight long-context datasets at 128K length, it consistently improved evidence utilization and achieved the best average rank on Qwen3-4B, Qwen3-8B, and Llama3-8B.

title: ElephantAgent: Contextual State Continuity in Agentic Systems
blurb: ElephantAgent is a protocol that enforces Contextual State Continuity to defend agentic systems against tool and memory poisoning attacks. Before each query, it recomputes a digest of the agent's security-critical contextual state and verifies it against an authorized digest stored in a linearizable ledger backed by replicated trusted hardware, with Historical Traceability for post-hoc audit and recovery to a known-good state.

title: Mitigating Package Hallucinations in Large Language Models via Model Editing
blurb: This paper introduces BOUND, a lightweight model editing framework that reduces package hallucinations, where LLMs generate non-existent package names that attackers can register as malicious supply-chain payloads. Using risk-aware localization and LoRA adapters with a boundary-aware objective, BOUND cut package-level hallucination rates by 79.9% on edit prompts and 65.4% on unseen ones while preserving valid recommendations.

title: Launch HN: Manufact (YC S25) – MCP Cloud
blurb: Manufact, a YC S25 startup, launched MCP Cloud, a platform for deploying MCP apps and servers to production via its open-source mcp-use SDK. It handles the full MCP lifecycle, including per-branch preview deployments, cross-client testing against ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini, marketplace submission checklists for the ChatGPT Apps Store and Claude Connectors, and production tracing through a browser-based Cloud Inspector.

title: Startup sues Palo Alto Networks' Koi Security, saying an AI-hallucinated report falsely linked it to Chinese espionage
blurb: A startup filed suit against Palo Alto Networks' Koi Security, alleging that an AI-hallucinated threat report falsely tied it to Chinese espionage. The complaint claims the fabricated findings, published as legitimate security research, damaged the company's reputation and business relationships, raising questions about liability when AI-generated errors make it into vendor threat intelligence.

title: OpenAI proposed donating 5% of its equity to a US sovereign wealth fund
blurb: OpenAI CEO Sam Altman proposed donating 5% of the company's equity to a US sovereign wealth fund, per the Financial Times, with other AI labs expected to contribute similar stakes. The plan aims to ease political blowback and fund citizen payouts, though the talks remain preliminary and would likely require congressional approval.

title: Agentic AI puts $234B in enterprise SaaS spending at risk, Gartner says
blurb: Gartner estimates that agentic AI puts up to $234 billion in enterprise application software spending at risk by 2030, roughly 20% of SaaS spending, as agents bypass human users and interact with business systems directly through APIs. The shift, which Gartner calls "agentic arbitrage," breaks the link between user growth and vendor revenue and pushes CIOs to scrutinize contract terms that may restrict autonomous third-party use.

title: Google launches Nano Banana 2 Lite and Gemini Omni Flash
blurb: Google released Nano Banana 2 Lite and Gemini Omni Flash, adding a faster image model via the Gemini API and a video generation model in public preview through Google AI Studio. Nano Banana 2 Lite generates images from text in four seconds at $0.034 per 1,000 images, while Omni Flash produces and conversationally edits up to 10 seconds of video at $0.10 per second.

title: Sources: Anthropic moves to close loopholes that let Chinese companies like Ant use its models via workarounds such as cloud providers and overseas subsidiaries (Financial Times)
blurb: Anthropic is moving to close loopholes that let Chinese companies like Ant Group access its models through workarounds, including third-party cloud providers and overseas subsidiaries, according to the Financial Times. The tightening reflects growing pressure to enforce usage restrictions on Chinese firms that circumvent direct sales bans by routing access through intermediaries and foreign entities.

title: ElevenLabs is in talks for a $22B valuation, doubling its price tag five months after its last raise: report
blurb: ElevenLabs is in early talks for a secondary share sale that would value the London-based AI voice company at roughly $22B, according to a Bloomberg report. The tender offer, expected to close by September, would double the $11B valuation from its February 2026 Series D without raising new capital, letting employees and other shareholders sell stock while the business keeps its cash.

title: Combating Textual Noise and Redundancy: Entropy-Aware Dense Visual Token Pruning
blurb: This paper introduces Entropy-Aware Dense Pruning (EADP), a visual token pruning framework that accelerates VLMs by filtering textual noise with statistical entropy, then casting token selection as a submodular maximization problem with a spatial prior rather than naive Top-K. The method preserves fine-grained visual cues under tight token budgets, reporting state-of-the-art accuracy-efficiency trade-offs on challenging multimodal benchmarks.

title: OrbitQuant: Data-Agnostic Quantization for Image and Video Diffusion Transformers
blurb: OrbitQuant is a data-agnostic post-training quantizer for diffusion transformers that skips per-checkpoint calibration by quantizing in a rotated, normalized basis where a single codebook covers all timesteps, prompts, and layers. Across FLUX.1, Z-Image-Turbo, Wan 2.1, and CogVideoX it sets state-of-the-art low-bit PTQ results and pushes image diffusion transformers to W2A4 with usable generation quality.

title: VisionAId: An Offline-First Multimodal Android Assistant for People with Visual Impairment, Featuring Personalized Object Retrieval
blurb: VisionAId is an Android app that turns a commodity smartphone into a real-time visual assistant for people with visual impairment, running six on-device models through ONNX Runtime with optional cloud LLM narration. A few-shot pipeline lets users photograph a personal object from several angles, then relocate that specific instance with AR markers, spatial audio, and haptics.

title: CheckRLM: Effective Knowledge-Thought Coherence Checking in Retrieval-Augmented Reasoning
blurb: CheckRLM is a framework that improves the factual reliability of Reasoning Language Models by checking and correcting errors in their reasoning chains at inference time. It extracts factual claims to localize knowledge inconsistencies, then uses retrieval-augmented generation to make minimal-cost corrections, outperforming existing baselines at mitigating error accumulation in long-horizon reasoning.

title: Unlocking Speech-Text Compositional Powers: Instruction-Following Speech Language Models without Instruction Tuning
blurb: SpeechCombine is an instruction-following speech language model trained without any instruction tuning, using a single round of speech pre-training on 30k hours of data. Starting from a text LLM, the authors continuously pre-train on speech, then add the weight difference between the instruction-tuned and base text models, transferring instruction-following to the speech domain without massive speech datasets.

title: OpenSafeIntent: Evaluating Intent-Calibrated Safe Completion Across Dual-Use Prompt Sets
blurb: OpenSafeIntent is a benchmark that pairs benign, dual-use, and malicious variants of the same task to test whether models calibrate assistance as intent shifts rather than appearing safe on average. Across a broad model suite, the authors find that prompt-level safety masks failures: dual-use behavior is brittle under paraphrase, high-level answers on risky topics are unreliable, and reframing ambiguous requests into safer tasks lowers boundary crossings.

title: AgentFlow: Building Agent Dependency Graphs for Static Analysis of Agent Programs
blurb: AgentFlow is the first static analysis framework for recovering agent dependencies from LLM agent programs, representing agents, prompts, models, tools, and memory as typed nodes in an Agent Dependency Graph. Evaluated across five frameworks and 5,399 real-world programs, it generates dependency-aware Agent Bills of Materials and uncovered 238 taint-style prompt-to-tool risks that existing AST-based tools miss.

title: Archer: Towards Agentic Review for Compiler Optimizations
blurb: Archer is an automated agentic code review tool for compiler optimizations that targets LLVM, constraining its analysis with obligations and a deterministic validation guard that admits only findings backed by executable evidence. Evaluated on 70 open and 328 closed LLVM PRs from the past two months, Archer flagged 21% of open PRs and 11% of closed PRs as introducing semantic bugs such as miscompilations.

title: When Do LLM Personas Support Visualization Design? A Cross-Model Study of Color Assignment and Chart Choice
blurb: This paper tests whether LLM personas produce stable personality effects in visualization design by running 43 Big Five profiles across GPT-4o-mini, GPT-4.1-mini, and GPT-5-mini on color assignment and chart-choice tasks. The authors find that personality-color coupling is highly model-dependent and that task context, not persona, drives top chart selection in 8 of 9 cases, positioning LLM personas as exploratory probes rather than substitutes for human participants.

title: Claude-real-video － any LLM can watch a video
blurb: claude-real-video is an open-source tool that lets Claude, ChatGPT, or Gemini analyze video by extracting only the frames that matter, detecting every scene change rather than sampling at a fixed interval, then deduplicating near-identical frames and transcribing audio locally. It runs entirely on-device using yt-dlp, ffmpeg, and Whisper, outputting a folder of frames, a transcript, and a manifest that any LLM can read without uploading the source.

title: Anthropic is discussing a new custom chip with Samsung
blurb: Anthropic is in talks with Samsung to explore developing its own custom AI chip, according to a report from The Information. The company has not decided what the chip will do or how powerful it will be, and told TechCrunch that a diversified stack spanning Google, Amazon, and Nvidia hardware remains central to its compute strategy.

title: Google’s AI buildout drove 37% increase in electricity use in 2025
blurb: Google reported that its electricity consumption rose 37% in 2025, the largest annual increase in company history, as its data centers consumed over 42 million megawatt-hours to support the AI buildout. Despite the jump, Google cut operational emissions by 2% through clean energy purchases, though supply chain emissions grew 25% and total ambition-based emissions rose 18%.

title: Investors sue Oracle, say it hid how shaky the OpenAI deal was
blurb: Investors sued Oracle, alleging it hid the fragility of its $300B cloud deal with OpenAI when selling $25B in notes in February. A Michigan pension fund's class action names Larry Ellison and Safra Catz, and argues Oracle concealed that OpenAI had missed revenue targets and privately doubted it could pay for the compute it committed to buy.

title: Online Safety Monitoring for LLMs
blurb: This paper studies a real-time safety monitor for LLM deployments that converts a verifier signal from an external model into an alarm by thresholding, with the threshold calibrated through risk control. On mathematical reasoning and red teaming datasets, the authors show this simple design is competitive with more advanced monitors based on sequential hypothesis testing.

title: Mark Zuckerberg tells staff that AI agents haven’t progressed as quickly as he’d hoped
blurb: Mark Zuckerberg told Meta staff at an internal town hall that AI agent development had not accelerated as executives had expected, and that the upside of the company's AI-focused restructuring had not yet come to fruition. The comments follow layoffs of roughly 8,000 employees and reassignment of 7,000 more to AI groups, as Meta prepares to spend as much as $145B on AI infrastructure this year.

title: Cloudflare changes AI crawler access rules
blurb: Cloudflare introduced controls that let website owners set separate policies for AI crawlers across three categories: Search, Agent, and Training, available on all plans including the Free tier. Starting September 15, 2026, new domains will block Training and Agent crawlers by default on pages that display ads, while Search crawlers stay allowed.

title: Cloudflare will filter out web crawlers that serve AI companies
blurb: Cloudflare will begin automatically blocking mixed-use web crawlers that index sites for search while also scraping content for AI agents and training, defaulting new and free accounts on September 15, 2026 to allow search but block training and agent use on pages with ads. It also renamed its Pay Per Crawl feature to Pay Per Use, which pays site owners when their content appears in AI chatbot answers, starting with Ceramic.AI and You.com.

title: Crusoe in Talks to Raise $3 Billion in Round That May Triple Firm’s Value
blurb: Crusoe is in talks to raise about $3B in a funding round that could triple its valuation, according to people familiar with the matter. The data center startup supplies AI computing power to customers including Meta and Oracle, and the raise would fund further buildout as demand for AI infrastructure capacity climbs.

title: What LLM Agents Say When No One Is Watching: Social Structure and Latent Objective Emergence in Multi-Agent Debates
blurb: This paper introduces a dual-channel debate framework that compares what LLM agents say publicly against off-the-record responses recorded but never shown to other participants. Across 10 models and 3 scenarios, alignment-inducing social settings pushed the targeted agent's public-vs-private decision divergence from a 3% baseline to roughly 40%, with some off-the-record responses attributing the public accommodation to career risk or sponsorship pressure.

title: Text-Driven 3D Indoor Scene Synthesis in Non-Manhattan Environments
blurb: SPG-Layout is a text-driven framework for generating physically plausible 3D indoor scenes in non-Manhattan environments, where non-orthogonal spatial relationships cause prior LLM-based methods to produce high geometric violations. It uses statistical priors of object distributions during training and a hierarchical strategy that places large objects first, and outperforms existing methods across both Manhattan and non-Manhattan settings on a new 500-environment benchmark.

title: Towards Robustness against Typographic Attack with Training-free Concept Localization
blurb: This paper introduces a training-free method to defend CLIP-based vision encoders against typographic attacks, where irrelevant text in an image biases representations toward lexical meaning over visual semantics. Using mechanistic interpretability and circuit mining, the authors isolate the Vision Transformer attention heads that encode lexical information and apply targeted weight adjustments that outperform supervised defenses and improve Visual Question Answering accuracy under attack on RIO-Bench.

title: HERMES: A Multi-Granularity Labeling Substrate for Pre-training Data Mixtures
blurb: HERMES is a data-derived labeling system for pre-training corpora that annotates each document once into a coarse-to-fine code, using a Learned Semantic Transform plus 3-stage residual vector quantization, with prefix length controlling granularity up to roughly 130k cells. On a 1B-parameter, 25B-token run, the hierarchy exposed a mixing rule that lifted a 16-task capability average by 0.0253 at one resolution but lost its edge at the next finer level.

title: Privacy-Preserving and Verifiable Approximate Distributed Coded Computing
blurb: This paper presents a model-agnostic framework for adversary-resistant distributed learning that jointly handles privacy leakage and malicious participants across both federated and decentralized settings. It combines GPBACC, a privacy-enhancing coded computing technique, with robust aggregation and lightweight decode-and-compare verification, and an attack-driven evaluation shows reduced privacy leakage and improved resilience against active adversaries without a trusted aggregator.

title: Synthetic Contact with AI Reduces Cross-Partisan Animosity
blurb: Across five preregistered studies with 3,960 US partisans, this paper finds that brief conversations with AI chatbots representing the opposing party reduce cross-partisan animosity and correct misperceptions that fuel division. A single ten-minute chat warmed affect and made participants six percentage points more likely to seek a real conversation with an opposing partisan, though most of the warmth effect faded within a week.

title: The fall of the theorem economy
blurb: The author argues that mathematics' real product is clarity and understanding, not theorems, so AI that automates proof-writing threatens only the least valuable part of the work. Drawing on his own unpublished results, he contends the hard part was always conjecturing and building the conceptual framework, while writing up the proof was mechanical drudgery. His conclusion is that AI could collapse the "theorem economy" of formal publication while leaving the actual intellectual core of math largely intact.

title: Using LLMs to Analyze Spark SQL Plans: A Practical Approach to Debugging Long-Running Jobs
blurb: This post from Expedia's engineering team presents a workflow that feeds Spark SQL query plans to LLMs to diagnose why data jobs run slowly. The approach parses physical and logical plan output, surfaces issues like skewed joins and unnecessary shuffles, and turns dense execution plans into actionable debugging suggestions for engineers.

title: The website of the future may assemble itself for every visitor
blurb: Adobe is experimenting with "agentic sites" that assemble a personalized page around each visitor's inferred intent in real time. Principal Scientist Carlos Sanchez demonstrated a system that reads browsing and search signals, sorts them into an intent category like exploring or preparing to purchase, and uses an LLM to compose the page. Rather than inventing content, it retrieves from the site's existing material as a grounding corpus, moving personalization past predefined segments toward what Adobe calls an "audience of one.

title: Nvidia floats double-dipping datacenter financing scheme
blurb: Nvidia floated a datacenter financing structure that would let it effectively get paid twice on the same capacity, once by supplying the GPUs and again through its stake in the financing that funds their purchase. The Register frames the arrangement as circular, raising questions about how much of the current AI infrastructure boom rests on Nvidia recycling its own revenue back into customer buildouts.

title: Meta reportedly plans to rent out its AI compute, sending AI stocks tumbling — 'Meta Compute' would put company in direct competition with AWS
blurb: Meta is reportedly building a cloud business called Meta Compute to sell excess AI computing capacity, according to Bloomberg, either by hosting access to its models in an AWS Bedrock-style arrangement or renting raw GPU capacity like neoclouds. The move would pit Meta against AWS, Google Cloud, and Azure, but markets hit its neocloud suppliers hardest, with CoreWeave falling 10.8% and Nebius dropping 12.4%.

title: Human Capital, Not Model Benchmarks, Predicts Hybrid Intelligence in Forecasting
blurb: This paper uses Polymarket as an externally resolved benchmark to show that the value of human-AI collaboration in forecasting depends on measurable human capital, not model benchmarks. Hybrid performance was trimodal: most people either deferred to the model or used it to rubber-stamp a prior guess, while a minority engaged in genuine complementary reasoning and matched or beat the market. Collaborative traits like perspective-taking, intellectual humility, and curiosity, rather than raw cognitive ability, distinguished who reached that mode. The findings are preliminary but statistically robust.

title: Neuron-Aware Data Selection for Annotation-Free LLM Self-Distillation
blurb: Neuron-OPSD is an annotation-free self-distillation framework that uses a model's internal neuron activations to guide both training-data selection and teacher-context construction, then trains via on-policy distillation with no ground-truth labels at any stage. Across specialized-domain benchmarks it improves in-domain performance while preserving cross-domain generalization and avoiding the calibration collapse seen in prior SFT-, GRPO-, and reward-based baselines.

title: AIEWF Daily Dispatch: The great loops debate and the state of AI engineering
blurb: The AI Engineer World's Fair closed with a debate over whether autonomous "loops" that let agents write and iterate on code are viable now or still outrunning the engineering discipline. Advocates Geoffrey Huntley and Ian Livingstone argued loops are inevitable and already replacing hand-written code, while skeptics Dex Horthy and Greg Pstrucha countered that the hype exceeds proven reliability and that token-heavy agentic loops aren't economically sustainable.

title: Claude Fable 5 isn’t permanently leaving subscriptions, Anthropic says
blurb: Anthropic said Claude Fable 5 will leave subscription plans after July 7 and shift to usage-credit billing, but the change is temporary. A Claude Code lead engineer clarified the company aims to restore Fable 5 as a standard part of Pro, Max, Team, and Enterprise plans once demand-driven capacity constraints ease.

title: Amazon’s Mechanical Turk to stop accepting new customers – and not even AI can save it
blurb: Amazon will stop accepting new customers for Mechanical Turk, its long-running marketplace for human microtask labor. The move signals that demand for the platform's crowdsourced data work has faded as AI took over many of the labeling and annotation tasks it once handled.

title: AWS raises AgentCore runtime quotas by up to 5x to help enterprises scale AI agents
blurb: AWS increased key Amazon Bedrock AgentCore runtime quotas by up to fivefold, raising default concurrent sessions to 5,000 in its two US East and West regions and 2,500 elsewhere, up from 1,000 and 500. The higher limits let enterprises move multi-agent systems from pilots to production without the quota-increase requests that often slow deployments, while likely driving more underlying compute consumption.

title: Blackstone-owned QTS abandons planned world’s largest data center campus after years of lawsuits — 2,100-acre Virginia Digital Gateway project dies over a newspaper-notice technicality
blurb: QTS, the Blackstone-owned data center developer, terminated its portion of Virginia's 2,100-acre Digital Gateway project by withdrawing its final appeal to the state Supreme Court on July 2, ending years of litigation over what would have been the world's largest data center campus. The 22-million-square-foot complex near Manassas National Battlefield Park collapsed after courts voided the county's rezoning approval over improper public notices.

title: Sources: Anthropic's bankers have hired UK law firm Freshfields to advise on its IPO; it also advised on Google's acquisition of Wiz and ServiceNow's Armis deal (The Information)
blurb: Anthropic's bankers have hired UK law firm Freshfields to advise on its planned IPO, according to sources cited by The Information. Freshfields previously advised on Google's acquisition of Wiz and ServiceNow's Armis deal, signaling that Anthropic is assembling the legal infrastructure for a public offering.

title: EquiLibre Technologies Raises Series A at €438M Valuation to Scale AI Trading Agents
blurb: EquiLibre Technologies raised a Series A at a €438M valuation to expand its reinforcement-learning trading agents and build one of the largest AI compute clusters in Central and Eastern Europe. Founded by the DeepMind researchers behind DeepStack, the Prague startup's agents trade billions of dollars daily across the S&P 500 and Nasdaq in partnership with Tower Research Capital.

title: G-RRM: Guiding Symbolic Solvers with Recurrent Reasoning Models
blurb: This paper introduces G-RRM, a neuro-symbolic method that uses symbol-equivariant Recurrent Reasoning Models to generate solution proposals that guide classical constraint solvers like backtracking and SAT-based methods. Gains hold only when the search space is large and the solver can overwrite bad neural hints: on 9×9 Sudoku, guidance sped up backtracking by 33.3× and Glucose 4.1 by 1.70×, while hint-respecting CaDiCaL saw no improvement.

title: EvoPolicyGym: Evaluating Autonomous Policy Evolution in Interactive Environments
blurb: EvoPolicyGym is a benchmark that measures how autonomous agents iteratively improve executable policies across 16 compact interactive RL environments under a fixed interaction budget. GPT-5.5 posted the strongest aggregate rank and top-two performance on every environment, and the benchmark adds trajectory-level diagnostics that show how agents allocate budget and convert feedback into parametric tuning.

title: Transformer Geometry Observatory TGO-II: Representational Similarity Observatory
blurb: TGO-II is a representation geometry framework that tracks how Vision Transformer (ViT-Small/16) internal representations evolve during supervised training, using CKA, SVCCA, TwoNN intrinsic dimensionality, and token covariance analysis. The authors find that CKA and SVCCA decline while intrinsic dimensionality rises, indicating that layer specialization and manifold expansion emerge together without the tokens decoupling.

title: Stable Self-Modulating Quantum Fast-Weight Programmers with Bounded Memory Gates
blurb: This paper stabilizes Self-Modulating Quantum Fast-Weight Programmers by bounding the old-state gate with a tanh function, removing long-sequence divergence.

title: Challenges and Recommendations for LLMs-as-a-Judge in Multilingual Settings and Low-Resource Languages
blurb: This paper audits how LLM-as-a-Judge is used to evaluate multilingual and low-resource language tasks, finding that only 33 of 650 ACL Anthology papers mentioning the method focus on those settings. The analysis surfaces inconsistent evaluation outcomes, a tendency to overtrust judge scores where LLMs have weak language proficiency, and near-universal reliance on a single judge model, closing with recommendations for more reliable multilingual evaluation.

title: From Forgeries to Foundation Models: A Systematic Survey of Identity Document Attack and Detection
blurb: This survey provides the first unified threat model covering presentation attacks, digital injection, and GenAI-driven synthesis for identity document forgery, tracing detection methods from rule-based heuristics through foundation models and few-shot frameworks. A systematic audit of 2019-2025 datasets exposes a persistent gap between benchmarks and deployment, with even the strongest public models scoring APCER above 25% on unseen synthesized ID cards under security-oriented conditions.

title: AgenticDataBench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Data Agents
blurb: AgenticDataBench is a benchmark for evaluating LLM-based data agents on realistic data science tasks spanning 15 vertical domains, including 5 real-world B2B use cases from a leading fintech company. It scores agents at the level of individual data science skills, recurring operational patterns extracted from Stack Overflow solutions via skill-aligned hierarchical clustering, yielding fine-grained insights into where agents succeed and fail.

title: AI can't be listed as inventor on patent applications, Japan's top court rules
blurb: Japan's Supreme Court ruled that an AI system cannot be named as an inventor on a patent application, holding that only a natural person qualifies as an inventor under current law. The decision aligns Japan with similar rulings in the US, UK, and Europe over the DABUS AI system, leaving inventions generated autonomously by AI without a clear path to patent protection.

title: Show HN: CLI tool for detecting non-exact code duplication with embedding models
blurb: Slopo is a CLI tool that detects non-exact code duplication by computing an embedding for every code unit and flagging pairs whose embeddings sit close together, targeting similar snippets spread far apart across a codebase. It supports Python, TypeScript, JavaScript, Java, Kotlin, C#, Go, and Rust, and outputs clusters ranked by similarity and distance that an AI coding agent can confirm as real duplicates before refactoring.

title: Tesla starts testing its production Cybercab without steering wheel or pedals in Austin
blurb: Tesla began testing a production Cybercab with no steering wheel or pedals on public roads in Austin, its first vehicle built without any manual driving controls, with a safety monitor in the passenger seat. The two-seat robotaxi relies solely on cameras for perception, targets a retail price under $30,000, and arrives as NHTSA moves to drop the federal brake-pedal requirement for automated-only vehicles.

title: How We Built DEmate: Taming LLMs for Data Engineering at Meta
blurb: Meta's engineering team shared how it built DEmate, an internal system for applying LLMs to data engineering tasks. The post covers the guardrails and orchestration used to make model output reliable for production data workflows, addressing the accuracy and consistency problems that arise when LLMs generate pipeline code and transformations at scale.

title: SoftBank enters the rent-a-GPU race as America looks for support for AI training
blurb: SoftBank is entering the GPU rental market, offering AI training capacity from the 10 GW US server farm it is building. The move gives the Japanese conglomerate a way to monetize that infrastructure as American AI labs increasingly hunt for compute to support large-scale training runs.

title: The AI Trade Is Losing One of Its Key Signals
blurb: Per-unit AI pricing is drifting lower even as capital keeps pouring into the sector, eroding a metric investors have relied on to gauge whether the buildout will pay off. The decline removes a key signal for the AI trade at a moment when markets are already uneasy about returns on hundreds of billions in spending.

title: Hong Kong Rises as Crucial Node in Asia’s $2 Trillion AI Trade
blurb: Hong Kong has become a critical conduit for high-tech products moving in and out of China, emerging as a key node in a $2 trillion network of Asian trade driven by the AI boom. The city's role as a gateway is being cemented by surging chip flows, tightening its position at the center of the region's AI supply chain.

title: Chip Industry Urges US to Avoid Moves That Distort Memory Market
blurb: The Semiconductor Industry Association warned the Trump administration against intervening on memory chip prices or production capacity, arguing that such moves would deepen a historic supply squeeze driven by AI demand. The group cautioned that government attempts to influence the market could distort pricing and worsen the shortage rather than ease it.

title: Trump Says He Wants AI Guardrails, But ‘As Little as Possible’
blurb: President Trump said he supports some standards for AI but wants regulation kept to "as little as possible" to avoid rules that could slow American companies. He framed the light-touch approach as necessary to keep US firms competitive with China in the global AI race.

title: Beyond Adam: SOAP and Muon for Faster, Label-Efficient Training of Machine Learning Interatomic Potentials
blurb: This paper systematically compares matrix-structured optimizers, including Muon, SOAP, and the hybrid SOAP-Muon, against Adam for training NequIP and Allegro machine learning interatomic potentials. SOAP and SOAP-Muon consistently improve both convergence speed and final accuracy, with the largest gains under partial force supervision, suggesting optimizer choice is an overlooked design axis for MLIPs.

title: TestEvo-Bench: An Executable and Live Benchmark for Test and Code Co-Evolution
blurb: TestEvo-Bench is a live benchmark of test and code co-evolution tasks mined from 152 Java projects, with tracks for generating new tests and updating failing ones against real commit histories. Its current snapshot holds 746 generation and 509 update tasks, where top agents pairing Claude Opus 4.7 and Gemini 3.1 Pro reached 77.5% and 74.6% success but dropped sharply on recent tasks and under tight cost limits.

title: Neuron-Aware Active Few-Shot Learning for LLMs
blurb: NeuFS is an active few-shot learning framework that selects annotation samples using LLMs' internal neuron activation patterns rather than output-level signals like predictive entropy or embedding similarity. Its dual-criteria strategy uses neuron patterns to ensure demonstration diversity while prioritizing samples the model tends to hallucinate on, outperforming existing AFSL baselines on reasoning and text classification across three datasets.

title: ACID: Action Consistency via Inverse Dynamics for Planning with World Models
blurb: ACID is a decision-time planning framework for action-conditioned world models that adds cycle action consistency, requiring that an inverse dynamics model recover each conditioned action from its predicted transition. Across four world models and six tasks spanning rigid and deformable manipulation, articulated control, and visual navigation, ACID improves planning and matches baseline accuracy with substantially less planning compute.

title: Extreme Adaptive Transformer for Time Series Forecasting
blurb: This paper introduces the Extreme-Adaptive Transformer (Exformer), a forecasting framework that explicitly models both normal and rare extreme events in skewed time series like hydrologic streamflow. Its extreme-adaptive attention combines three sparse components, Local, Stride, and Extreme, and beat state-of-the-art baselines on 3-day forecasts across four real-world streamflow datasets.

title: Bringing Agentic Search to Earth Observation Data Discovery
blurb: This paper presents an agentic search system, deployed as a public service, that turns natural-language geoscience queries into matching NASA Earth observation datasets and tools like Worldview and Giovanni. The authors release NASA-EO-Bench, an open benchmark of 47k query-dataset pairs, where a neural scorer fused with BM25 lifts Recall@10 and MRR over 5x, and a zero-shot agentic reranking stage adds a further 28% MRR gain.

title: One More Time: Revisiting Neural Quantum States from a Reinforcement Learning Perspective
blurb: This paper recasts neural quantum state training as policy-gradient optimization, introducing PWO, a trust-region method that scales NQS to a 1.5B-parameter model.

title: Personality Without Persons? A Psychometric Critique of Big Five Testing in Large Language Models
blurb: This paper argues that Big Five personality inventories, increasingly used to characterize and govern LLMs, do not measure anything equivalent to human personality in models. Administering the best-validated inventory to 244 models across 49 families, the authors found low variance between models (3% of total score variance) and a collapse of four of the five factors into one (r ≥ .92). Alignment training also shifted scores toward socially desirable traits, suggesting human frameworks produce misleading model comparisons.

title: Skill engineering and the case against one-shot AI design
blurb: Paul Bakaus argues that AI agents should not do one-shot design, and that the emerging craft of "skill engineering" works best when humans stay in the loop to steer results. In this Latent Space interview, he explains Impeccable, his open-source design skills system that gives coding agents a vocabulary like "bolder," "quieter," or "denser" to refine interfaces incrementally. He also covers cross-harness differences between Claude Code, Cursor, and Codex, and using MoE-style routing inside a skill to conserve tokens.

title: SoftBank unveils plans to enter the US neocloud business with SB Neo
blurb: SoftBank announced SB Neo, a US neocloud business that will rent access to AI chips to hyperscalers and other compute-hungry customers starting in fiscal 2027. SoftBank Corp. will hold 51% and SoftBank Group 49%, with the venture targeting roughly 10 gigawatts of AI compute capacity for US customers by 2030, building on a GPU cloud beta it has run in Japan since April.

title: Generalization in offline RL: The structure is more important than the amount of pessimism
blurb: This paper challenges the assumption that overly pessimistic value functions block generalization in offline reinforcement learning, showing instead that generalization in contextual MDPs depends on whether the pessimistic structure respects the optimal solution's symmetries. The authors prove a symmetric but overly pessimistic value function can generalize better than a mildly pessimistic asymmetric one, and validate applying data augmentation as a consistency loss during policy extraction using IQL and CQL on a rotationally symmetric reacher task.

title: CADENZA in Action: Breaking the Monolith with Intent-Dependent Plan Spaces for Semantic Queries
blurb: CADENZA is a semantic operator optimizer that compiles a natural-language intent into decomposed steps, selects a physical implementation for each, and tunes their parameters under user-specified quality, latency, and cost preferences. Unlike optimizers that treat models like LLMs and embedding models as monolithic units, it lets users explore how one intent yields different winning plans as those preferences change.

title: Janus: a Playground for User-Involved Agentic Permission Management
blurb: Janus is a playground system for implementing and evaluating how users participate in permission management for AI agents that autonomously execute tool calls. The authors build six permission assistants across the design space and test them against three scenarios and three synthetic responders, finding that user input meaningfully strengthens privacy and security but no single design wins across all contexts.

title: Singapore cops seize $42 million mansion, freeze $772k bank account of suspected Nvidia AI GPU smugglers — individuals alleged to have illegally exported data center servers to China charged with fraud, money laundering
blurb: Singapore charged four individuals with fraud and money laundering for smuggling Nvidia AI GPUs and data center servers into China through the country's port as a transshipment point. Authorities seized a $42M mansion and froze a $772k bank account, framing the case as fraud over misrepresented server end users to Dell, Supermicro, and Asus since US export controls don't apply under Singapore law.

title: Zuckerberg Says Meta's AI Agents Lag as Wang Claims Model Caught GPT-5.5
blurb: Zuckerberg told a July 2 Meta town hall that the company's AI agent work hadn't "accelerated in the way we expected" over the past four months, and that its restructuring bets "haven't come to fruition yet," with a payoff expected in three to six months. In the same meeting, AI chief Alexandr Wang claimed Meta's next model had caught up with OpenAI's GPT-5.5 on benchmarks, as Meta shares closed down 4.9%.

title: Hardware-Enforced Semantic Coordination for Safety-Critical Real-Time Autonomous Systems
blurb: This paper proposes a hardware-enforced coordination architecture that maps selected semantics from the Topic-Based Communication Space Petri Net framework onto FPGA primitives for safety-critical autonomous systems. The design enforces temporal synchronization, semantic gating, authorization constraints, and bounded coordination directly in hardware, keeping semantic reasoning adaptive in software while making the coordination layer deterministic.

title: Understanding the Robustness of Distributed Self-Supervised Learning Frameworks Against Non-IID Data
blurb: This paper's theoretical analysis shows Masked Image Modeling is more robust to non-IID data in distributed self-supervised learning than contrastive learning.

title: LIME: Learning Intent-aware Camera Motion from Egocentric Video
blurb: This paper introduces LIME, a vision-language model that generates language-conditioned camera motion, predicting a relative target camera pose from an RGB observation and a free-form natural-language intent. It mines multi-intention supervision from egocentric video and pairs an auto-regressive observation-gain output with a continuous flow-matching pose head, letting robots learn intent-aware active perception from passive human recordings.

title: Know Your Source: A Public Knowledge Store for Media Background Checks
blurb: This paper introduces MEDIAREF, a publicly available knowledge store of web-sourced documents that enables reproducible, low-cost evaluation of media background check generation across 200 media sources. Media background checks assess the credibility of evidence sources for automated fact-checking, and the authors show that MEDIAREF supports higher-quality generation than costly proprietary search APIs across both automatic and qualitative evaluation.

title: Unveiling the Non-Monotonic Effect of Privacy on Generalization under Byzantine Robustness
blurb: This paper proves that the established trilemma between Byzantine robustness, local differential privacy, and optimization error does not carry over to generalization error, which instead depends on the privacy regime. In the high-noise regime stronger privacy reduces generalization error with no robustness tension, while in the low-noise regime that tension returns and more privacy degrades generalization, a non-monotonic behavior the authors explain via matching bounds on algorithmic stability.

title: Guiding Human Validation of LLM-Generated Code via Verifiable Literate Programming
blurb: This paper presents verifiable literate programming (VLP), a human-in-the-loop framework that inserts an unambiguous natural-language documentation layer between prompts and LLM-generated code so users at any skill level can review and validate what the model produced. VLP flags suspicious documentation lines for focused review and derives formal properties checked against the code via model checking, raising pass@1 from a 28.7%-73.2% range to 65.4%-93.5%.

title: An Exploratory Study on LLM-Generated Code and Comments in Code Repositories
blurb: This paper studies code and comments flagged as likely LLM-generated across active company- and community-maintained repositories from 2021 to 2025, using detector-based proxy analysis. Detected LLM-generated code decreased over time, clustered in test cases, and showed substantial intra-repository cloning, while flagged comments stayed stable but often lacked grammatical correctness. Company-maintained repositories had higher proportions of both, and only a small share of human-labeled bugs were associated with LLM-generated code.

title: Right to Local Intelligence
blurb: The Right to Intelligence campaign argues that people should be free to download, own, run, study, modify, and share open AI models, defining local AI as a model you can run and repair on your own machine without asking a platform to stay online. The manifesto pushes back against potential licensing requirements for local AI, while affirming that harms like fraud, CSAM, and nonconsensual deepfakes should remain illegal and enforced.

title: ArXiv's Next Chapter
blurb: arXiv will spin out from Cornell University on July 1, 2026, becoming an independent nonprofit after 25 years hosted at the institution. The organization said its mission stays unchanged, remaining free to read and submit to, and expects authors and readers to notice little disruption during the transition.

title: An interview with Sriram Krishnan, who says "there will not be an FDA for AI" under Trump, blames the AI backlash on the industry's "doomer" messaging, and more (Financial Times)
blurb: White House AI advisor Sriram Krishnan said in an interview that "there will not be an FDA for AI" under the Trump administration, ruling out a dedicated federal regulator for the technology. He blamed the public backlash against AI on the industry's "doomer" messaging, arguing that catastrophizing rhetoric has fueled opposition rather than the technology itself.

title: OpenAI offers feds a stake, Anthropic gets out of AI model jail and Meta wants to be a neocloud
blurb: This SiliconANGLE roundup covers a week of AI-industry moves: OpenAI reportedly floated giving the U.S. government a 5% stake, the Trump administration lifted export controls on two of Anthropic's most powerful models with new restrictions, and Meta signaled plans to sell cloud services until its own models need the compute. It also notes Etched launching with $800M for inference chips and South Korea's $584B initiative with Samsung and SK hynix.

title: Crunchbase Data: Global Startup Investment Hit Record $510B In H1 2026 As AI Boom Accelerates Funding And Exits
blurb: Global venture funding hit a record $510B in H1 2026, already surpassing the $440B invested in all of 2025 and topping the previous half-year peak set in H2 2021. OpenAI and Anthropic alone took $217B, 43% of the total, with close to a third of Q2 funding going to Anthropic's $65B raise. Exits came back at the same time, led by SpaceX's $1.77T IPO and its $60B acquisition of Cursor-maker Anysphere. Capital is concentrating into a handful of frontier names while the boom spreads into AI infrastructure, defense, robotics, and healthcare.

title: Vercel Took a 10-Person SDR Team Down to 1. The Whole Thing Costs $5,000 a Year. With Vercel’s COO Jeanne DeWitt Grosser.
