blurb: Vercel took lead qualification from a 10-person team down to roughly one and a quarter people, and the agent that did it runs about $5,000 a year in infrastructure and tokens against 10 salaries saved. The build method is the reusable part: a GTM engineer, a data scientist, and the single best subject-matter expert encode the function's best practice into workflows, with a human QAing every output until the expert runs out of feedback to give. The people who did the deterministic work moved up the stack, which is also how you sell this internally.

title: Sources: Alexandr Wang said Meta's model currently in training, codenamed Watermelon, matches GPT-5.5 and uses an "order of magnitude more compute than Avocado" (Business Insider)
blurb: Meta's next model, codenamed Watermelon, matches GPT-5.5 in training and runs on an order of magnitude more compute than its predecessor Avocado, according to Alexandr Wang. Meta is buying its way back to the frontier with raw compute after falling behind on the last generation, a strategy that only holds while capex keeps outpacing everyone else. The open question for founders building on Llama is whether parity with GPT-5.5 ships as a weights release you can run yourself or stays locked behind Meta's own products.

title: Microsoft launches its own AI deployment company with $2.5 billion commitment
blurb: Microsoft launched Microsoft Frontier Company, a new operating business backed by $2.5B and 6,000 engineers to run enterprise AI deployments on its existing tools. It joins a wave of forward-deployed engineering ventures announced in the past month, including a $1B AWS commitment two days earlier and joint efforts from OpenAI and Anthropic. Microsoft's edge is distribution: it has already placed engineers across much of the Fortune 500, and the launch cites early work with the London Stock Exchange Group, Unilever, Land O'Lakes, and Accenture. The frontier labs' real product is becoming the deployment, not the model.

title: Most AI Work Can Wait
blurb: Most teams building AI agents pick the model first and the architecture second, when the model choice should be the last decision. What matters is the router, a small piece of code that reads a task's classified intent and features to decide which tier handles it, so 70-80% of traffic runs on local models that cost nothing or async batch jobs that cut spend 90%. The real question is narrower than it looks: what fraction of work actually needs a real-time answer? Once the system can queue, surprisingly little.

title: Meta’s push into cloud computing means Wall Street has to prepare for lower margins
blurb: Meta is preparing to sell its excess computing power to outside customers, moving into a cloud infrastructure business that carries far slimmer margins than the ad business generating almost all its profit today. The bet is a direct answer to investors who have watched four straight quarterly stock declines and doubted Meta would ever earn a return on its $145B capex plan, part of it funded by a $25B bond sale. Wall Street welcomed the pivot, sending the stock up 9%, but a lucrative ad company is now volunteering to compete in a lower-margin market.

title: Kling Raises $2.8 Billion Amid Planned Spinoff From Kuaishou
blurb: Kuaishou's Kling raised $2.8B from investors as it prepares to spin off and separately list its AI video unit. The raise ranks among the largest for a video-generation business and gives Kling standalone capital to compete with OpenAI's Sora and Google's Veo. Spinning the unit out signals that incumbents now see AI video as a distinct business worth its own balance sheet and public listing, not a feature bolted onto a short-video app.

title: Nvidia offers to take a cut of AI cloud revenue on top of hardware sales in new optional financing vehicle — trades tokens for revenue cut
blurb: Nvidia introduced a revenue-sharing model that lets it double-dip on the same silicon, collecting its usual hardware margin plus an ongoing percentage of the cloud income that hardware generates. Cash-poor AI clouds get token credits today in exchange for a slice of their future sales, which helps them clear a financing gap lenders won't touch even against signed demand. A supplier already running roughly 75% gross margins is now reaching into its customers' income statements for a second helping, and first partners Sharon AI and Firmus have committed to a combined 210,000 GPUs.

title: Alibaba, Tencent Join $2.8 Billion Round for Kling AI
blurb: Chinese generative video keeps pulling capital, with Alibaba and Tencent joining a $2.8B round for Kuaishou's Kling AI that values the service at about $15B pre-money. Kling is one of China's most-used AI video products, and the raise funds an expansion of its video operations against a field that already includes OpenAI's Sora and Google's Veo. The signal for founders is that video generation is consolidating around a few heavily capitalized players, and the Chinese entrants now have the balance sheets to compete on model scale and distribution.

title: Trump gets OpenAI to offer US 5% stake, far lower than Sanders’ target
blurb: OpenAI is in early talks to give the US government a 5% stake, well below the larger share Senator Sanders has pushed for. Sam Altman argues that handing the public a financial stake is the best way to share AI's upside, and the administration has floated the same idea to Google and Meta, though neither has signed on. The backdrop is an AI hate wave: 70% of Americans don't want data centers nearby and half are more concerned than excited about AI, and both parties now read the sentiment as an electoral risk.

title: ElevenLabs in Talks on Tender Offer at $22 Billion Valuation
blurb: ElevenLabs is in early talks for a tender offer that would value the voice-AI startup at roughly $22 billion, letting employees cash out shares in a secondary sale. The round would nearly double the $6.6 billion valuation it reached in early 2025, a pace that tracks the money still chasing AI infrastructure over applications. Secondaries like this are becoming the default liquidity path as IPOs stay scarce, giving employees an exit while founders keep the company private and the valuation clock running.

title: OpenAI mulling giving US gov't a 5% stake in the company, days after Washington delayed GPT-5.6 — Altman reportedly wants every leading U.S. AI lab paying into an Alaska-style public fund
blurb: OpenAI has discussed handing the U.S. government a 5% stake, worth roughly $42.6 billion at its $852 billion valuation, with Altman proposing that every leading U.S. lab pay the same share into a fund modeled on Alaska's oil-dividend program. The talks are early and conceptual, and any deal would likely need an act of Congress. The administration has already run a version of this playbook, taking 9.9% of Intel and 15% of Nvidia and AMD's China chip revenue, and the news lands six days after Washington got OpenAI to delay GPT-5.6's public launch.

title: OpenAI proposed donating 5% of its equity to a US sovereign wealth fund
blurb: OpenAI proposed donating 5% of its equity to a US sovereign wealth fund, with other frontier labs expected to contribute similar stakes, in a move the FT frames as securing goodwill with the administration and blunting political blowback. The talks are preliminary and would likely need congressional approval, and they run alongside a more aggressive Sanders bill that would tax 50% of "systemically important" AI companies' stock into a public fund. The signal for founders is that equity in the biggest labs is becoming a political bargaining chip, and public-ownership structures are moving from think-piece to legislative draft.

title: Los agentes de IA ponen en riesgo 234.000 millones de dólares del gasto empresarial en SaaS, según Gartner
blurb: Gartner projects that agentic AI puts up to $234B of enterprise SaaS spend at risk by 2030, roughly 20% of the category, as agents interact with corporate apps directly through APIs instead of human users clicking through screens. The mechanism is what Gartner calls agentic arbitrage: agents complete tasks across multiple systems and return results, breaking the old link between user growth and vendor revenue. The buying test shifts too, from interface and UX to whether an agent can do everything a person does through the API, and whether the vendor's contract even permits autonomous use.

title: Agentic AI puts $234B in enterprise SaaS spending at risk, Gartner says
blurb: Gartner projects agentic AI puts up to $234B in enterprise software spending at risk by 2030, roughly 20% of SaaS, as agents bypass human users and hit systems directly through APIs. That breaks the link between seat growth and revenue that seat-based pricing and UX-led differentiation were built on. The near-term catch for buyers is contractual, not technical: many vendor terms restrict third-party autonomous use, so an AI strategy can be blocked by a clause already signed rather than by capability, making the API surface and the contract worth scrutinizing before the tech.

title: Microsoft unveils $2.5B ‘Frontier Company’ to embed AI engineers inside customers
blurb: Microsoft launched The Microsoft Frontier Company, a $2.5B, 6,000-person organization that embeds its own engineers inside customers to build and run AI systems on-site. Forward-deployed engineering, pioneered by Palantir two decades ago, is suddenly the enterprise AI land grab: Amazon committed $1B two days earlier, OpenAI stood up a $4B-backed Deployment Company, and Anthropic raised $1.5B with Goldman, Blackstone, and Hellman & Friedman. The labs and clouds are done selling tools and walking away, and are now competing to sit inside your operations and own the outcome.

title: ElevenLabs is in talks for a $22B valuation, doubling its price tag five months after its last raise: report
blurb: ElevenLabs is in early talks for a secondary sale that would value the AI voice company at roughly $22B, per Bloomberg, doubling its $11B Series D from February without raising a dollar of new capital. The mechanism matters: a tender offer lets employees and early shareholders cash out while the business keeps the money it already has, so the markup is pure demand, not dilution. Underneath it, ARR reportedly hit $500M in the first four months of 2026, up from $350M at the end of 2025, which is what secondary buyers are actually paying to get into.

title: IQM, Europe’s first public quantum company, admits the future of the tech is uncertain
blurb: IQM, a full-stack quantum company out of Finland, became Europe's first public quantum firm via a SPAC merger at a roughly $1.9B valuation, but the stock spent most of its debut below the IPO price. The lukewarm welcome traces partly to IQM's own prospectus, which warns that large-scale commercial traction "may never occur" because no one can say when quantum advantage arrives. Customer counts grew from eight in 2024 to 22 in 2025, yet demand won't scale until quantum chips outperform classical machines, so investors are still funding a timeline nobody can name.

title: Anthropic is discussing a new custom chip with Samsung
blurb: Anthropic is in talks with Samsung to build its own custom AI chip, moving from an April thought experiment to a serious effort to cut its dependence on Nvidia. The details are still open, with no decision yet on what the chip does, how powerful it is, or where it sits in the server. The move lands a week after OpenAI and Broadcom announced their own inference processor, and it fits the broader pattern of frontier labs building silicon to control cost and supply rather than rent it all from one vendor.

title: Amazon has deployed enough satellites to launch Leo service later this year
blurb: Amazon says it now has more than 390 satellites in orbit, enough to begin "initial service" of its Leo internet network later this year and start competing with SpaceX's Starlink. The gap is still enormous: Starlink launched in 2015 and runs roughly 10,000 satellites and more than 10 million subscribers, while Amazon is targeting a 7,700-satellite constellation that rocket shortages and a Blue Origin launchpad explosion have slowed. For founders, a second viable low-earth-orbit provider means connectivity in remote geographies stops depending on a single vendor's pricing and terms.

title: Mark Zuckerberg tells staff that AI agents haven’t progressed as quickly as he’d hoped
blurb: Meta cut about 8,000 jobs and reassigned 7,000 more into AI groups this year, but Zuckerberg just told staff at an internal town hall that agent development hasn't accelerated the way executives expected. He admitted the layoffs weren't as clean as they should have been and that the payoff from the AI-focused restructuring hasn't come to fruition yet, though he expects gains in the next three to six months. Meta is on track to spend as much as $145B on AI infrastructure this year, which sets the bar for what those improvements have to justify.

title: Finland’s IQM just proved a European deep tech company can list on Nasdaq without moving to the US
blurb: Finland's IQM began trading on Nasdaq on July 2 under the ticker IQMX, reaching the public market through a SPAC merger while staying headquartered in Espoo. The deal values IQM at roughly $1.8 billion pre-money and delivers a €337 million cash position, with existing Finnish backers like Tesi, Varma, and Elo holding their shares rather than cashing out. What gave IQM its leverage is revenue most quantum rivals can't show: €31 million in 2025 sales, a €67 million order backlog, and 23 quantum computers already sold, proving a European deep tech company can reach US capital without relocating.

title: A New Look at AI's Impact on Jobs: Firm-Level AI Spending and Workforce Adjustment
blurb: A new Ramp analysis uses firm-level card-spending data to link how much a company invests in AI to how it adjusts its workforce. The finding operators should sit with is that AI spending shows up in hiring plans before it shows up in headline layoff numbers, with the heaviest AI adopters slowing new hiring in the roles the tools touch first rather than cutting existing staff. The signal to watch is not the aggregate jobs report but the gap opening between firms scaling AI budgets and everyone else in how fast they staff up.

title: Autonomous drone startup Quantum Systems raises $1.2 billion as investors pile into defense
blurb: Quantum Systems raised $1.2B at an $8B valuation, one of a wave of defense startups drawing record capital this year. Defense tech has already pulled in $17.4B in 2026, up from $11.2B in all of 2025, with Anduril taking $5B, Shield AI $2B, and Saronic $1.8B. The German maker of autonomous land, air, and sea systems is profitable and battle-tested, running more than 19,000 missions in Ukraine last year, which is the signal investors are now paying for over pure early-stage bets.

title: Sources: Anthropic's bankers have hired UK law firm Freshfields to advise on its IPO; it also advised on Google's acquisition of Wiz and ServiceNow's Armis deal (The Information)
blurb: Anthropic's bankers have hired UK firm Freshfields, the same advisers behind Google's $32B Wiz acquisition and ServiceNow's Armis deal, to work on its IPO. Bringing in M&A-heavy counsel this early signals the offering is moving from rumor to process, positioning what would be one of the largest AI listings yet. For founders, it marks the first frontier lab testing whether public markets will underwrite a company still burning billions to stay at the frontier.

title: China Quant Funds Draw Billions as AI Trounces Human Traders
blurb: Chinese quant funds are pulling in billions as AI-driven systematic strategies outperform human traders, and the money is arriving faster than the funds can deploy it. The shift concentrates capital in a handful of firms whose edge is compute and data pipelines rather than star portfolio managers. For founders, it is another market where the AI-native operator scales headcount-light while the traditional competitor still staffs up to keep pace.

title: Jeff Bezos' family office backed five AI startups in June
blurb: Jeff Bezos' family office made five direct AI investments in June, roughly 10% of all family office dealmaking that month, making Bezos Expeditions the most active family office investor of the year with eight private deals so far. The bulk went to Prometheus, his own physical-product invention startup, which raised a $12B Series B at a $41B valuation and has now pulled in more than $18B to fund compute and datasets. The other four bets, spanning chemistry models, brain-inspired models, robotics, and spatial AI, show the ultra-rich treating frontier AI as the default place to park capital.

title: Global venture funding hits record $510B in first half as AI boom accelerates
blurb: Startups raised a record $510B in the first half of 2026, more than all of last year and past the previous half-year high of $375B set in late 2021. OpenAI and Anthropic alone took $217B, or 43% of the total, a level of concentration in two frontier labs that has no precedent. AI now pulls more than 70% of all startup capital, up from just under half a year ago, and the money is stacking into a handful of mega-rounds while the rest of the field competes for what's left.

title: Sources: Alibaba banned Claude Code internally and asked its employees to remove all Claude models from their work computers due to Anthropic security concerns (The Information)
blurb: Alibaba banned Claude Code internally and told employees to strip all Claude models from work machines, citing Anthropic security concerns, according to The Information. The move signals that geopolitical trust, not capability, is now shaping which frontier tools large firms will run. For founders building on US labs, the lesson is that your model provider is becoming a procurement risk in some markets, and enterprise buyers increasingly weigh where a model was trained and who controls it alongside price and benchmark scores.

title: 20VC x SaaStr: The Token ROI Crisis Comes for Everyone, Anthropic Wants Chinese Open Source Banned, and Microsoft Has Its Worst Month Since 2000
blurb: Companies quintupled their token spend in the first half of the year, and almost nobody can draw a clean line from that spend to a revenue lift. The trigger was Coinbase cutting AI costs 50% in a quarter while usage rose, done by routing away from frontier models toward open source. Boards are now tying spend approval to ROI, and even green-dashboard companies hitting plan are getting asked to justify the next token increase. If you sell software and can't show the lift, or at least the savings, that spend is about to get a much harder look.

title: Investors sue Oracle, say it hid how shaky the OpenAI deal was
blurb: Oracle's own investors sued the company, alleging it hid how shaky its $300B OpenAI deal was when it sold $25B in notes in February. The suit says Oracle touted a backlog that had ballooned from about $97B to more than $523B in a year without disclosing that nearly all of it traced to OpenAI, which had missed revenue targets and privately questioned whether it could pay. This is the AI boom's circular-financing worry filed as a legal document, and Oracle isn't alone, with Microsoft facing its own shareholder suit over AI spending.

title: SK hynix to invest $712.5 billion in South Korean operations — Cheongju NAND expansion, Yongin Semiconductor Cluster for DRAM detailed
blurb: SK hynix said it will invest KRW 1.1 quadrillion, roughly $712.5 billion, across its South Korean operations over the coming decades, with $389.3 billion going to the Yongin DRAM cluster and $259.5 billion to a new southwestern cluster. Only the $64 billion Cheongju expansion is detailed so far, funding a 3D NAND fab and more HBM packaging that won't come online until 2029 at the earliest. The scale signals that memory, not logic, is now the capacity bottleneck for the AI buildout, and the payoff sits years out.

title: Nvidia recruits longtime Microsoft sales leader Nick Parker with $40M+ pay package
blurb: Nvidia hired Nick Parker, a 26-year Microsoft veteran who ran its worldwide commercial sales, as executive vice president of worldwide field operations, with a package built around $1M base, a $5M signing bonus, and roughly $40M in equity. He takes over the operation selling Nvidia's chips to the world's largest companies, reporting directly to Jensen Huang and succeeding Jay Puri after 21 years. Talent is now moving toward whoever controls the AI supply chain, and the same week Microsoft launched a $2.5B program to embed its engineers inside customers.

title: Inside the race to power AI data centers with fusion energy — and the surprise detours along the way
blurb: Two Seattle-area fusion startups are racing to power AI data centers as surging demand and community backlash strain the grid. Helion Energy has $1.5B and a deal to sell fusion power to Microsoft by 2028, betting on being the first commercial fusion plant. Zap Energy, with $330M and DOE backing, is hedging by adding conventional nuclear fission for near-term revenue. The demand crunch is what turned a decades-long research goal into a funded commercial sprint.

title: Zoom snaps up Seattle startup Common Room to bolster AI-powered sales tools
blurb: Zoom acquired Seattle startup Common Room to push its Zoom Revenue Accelerator upstream, pairing Common Room's buyer-intelligence signals with the call data Zoom already captures. Common Room raised $52M from Index, Madrona, and Greylock and counted Notion and Pulumi among early customers; terms were not disclosed. The move fits a pattern where a $25B video company keeps bolting sales, service, and buyer-intent AI onto its core, betting revenue teams will consolidate onto one platform rather than stitch together point tools.

title: More deals, smaller rounds: European startup funding in June 2026
blurb: European startups closed 293 funding deals in June, up 14% from May, but total raised fell to €8.3B from €10.5B. The month traded mega-rounds for volume, with 16 companies clearing €100M and NEURA Robotics' up-to-$1.4B Series C the lone standout. Robotics led all sectors at €1.3B, and Germany topped the country table with €2.4B across 43 deals. The pattern points to broader, earlier capital deployment rather than concentrated bets, alongside rising cross-border M&A that is knitting Europe's tech ecosystem tighter.

title: IQM becomes first European quantum computing company to list on a major US exchange
blurb: IQM Quantum Computers listed on Nasdaq under IQMX, becoming the first European quantum computing company on a major US exchange, with a €337 million pro forma cash position. The company has sold 23 quantum computers worldwide, more than any other manufacturer, and runs full-stack superconducting systems at institutions like Oak Ridge National Laboratory, CINECA, and the Leibniz Supercomputing Center. The listing signals that quantum is shifting from research budgets to procurement, with buyers moving from exploration to owned infrastructure and a European vendor reaching the public markets ahead of its US rivals.

title: Quantum Systems signed a $1.2 billion round at a valuation of about $8 billion — and one of the investors co-leading it is Airbus, exactly the kind of defense prime the startup’s founder says his company could disrupt
blurb: Munich-based Quantum Systems raised $1.2B at roughly $8B, more than doubling its valuation, in a round co-led by Blackstone, Advent, and Airbus. The detail worth pausing on is Airbus: co-founder Florian Seibel calls his company a "neo prime" built to disrupt exactly the traditional defense primes Airbus embodies, and the incumbent invested anyway while deepening its strategic collaboration. The operational résumé is Ukrainian, with more than 19,000 missions flown in 2025, and the company says it is already profitable on triple-digit growth. When the incumbent funds its would-be disruptor, it is buying optionality, not conviction.

title: Crusoe in Talks to Raise $3 Billion in Round That May Triple Firm’s Value
blurb: Crusoe is in talks to raise about $3B in a round that could triple its valuation, on the strength of contracts to supply AI compute to Meta and Oracle. The capital chasing data center capacity is now flowing to the picks-and-shovels layer, where a company selling compute to the hyperscalers can triple its worth in a single round. For founders, it marks how much of the AI market's value is accruing to infrastructure suppliers rather than the application builders sitting on top of them.

title: Europe is coming for loot boxes, and the games industry is bracing
blurb: Europe is assembling a cluster of rules aimed at how games sell to minors, with loot boxes the main target, and the industry is bracing for the hit. Chance-based mechanics earned game companies about $23bn worldwide last year, PEGI now rates any game with loot boxes unsuitable for under-16s, and the EU's Digital Fairness Act, expected to pass next year, could ban them outright in games children can reach. Because companies change their games globally rather than region by region, strict European limits would dent sales far beyond the continent, and Roblox already shows the risk after age checks knocked 18% off its shares in May.

title: EquiLibre Technologies Raises Series A at €438M Valuation to Scale AI Trading Agents
blurb: EquiLibre Technologies, the Prague startup whose founders built DeepStack, the first AI to beat professionals at no-limit poker, raised a Series A at a €438M valuation, up from €122.8M a round earlier. Its reinforcement-learning agents now trade billions of dollars daily across the S&P 500 and Nasdaq alongside Tower Research, and the company says it has not posted a losing month since launch, though it has disclosed no performance data to back that. The capital goes into one of the largest AI compute clusters in Central and Eastern Europe.

title: AI can't be listed as inventor on patent applications, Japan's top court rules
blurb: Japan's Supreme Court ruled that an AI system cannot be named as an inventor on a patent application, holding that inventorship is reserved for humans. The decision aligns Japan with the US, UK, and Europe, all of which have rejected the same claim from the DABUS project. For founders building with generative models, it settles a practical question: patents flow to the people who direct the tools, not the tools themselves, so the human contribution to an AI-assisted invention is what determines who owns it.

title: Tesla saw a massive sales jump in the second quarter
blurb: Tesla delivered more than 480,000 vehicles in the second quarter, up over 120,000 from Q1 and its best Q2 ever by raw deliveries, easily beating Wall Street. The gains came from geographic expansion and cheaper versions of the Model 3, Model Y, and Cybertruck, not a broad market recovery. It's the strongest quarter since Q3 2025, but Tesla still has to reverse a two-year slide in overall sales, so the question is whether discounting new buyers into the funnel holds up once the cheaper trims stop being novel.

title: Overland AI lands Marine Corps deal worth nearly $20M to build self-driving military vehicles
blurb: Overland AI won a nearly $20M Marine Corps contract to build more than a dozen self-driving military vehicles, making it the first ground autonomy startup to hold a military production deal as prime contractor rather than a supplier under a larger defense firm. The Seattle company has raised over $140M, including a $100M round in February led by 8VC, and grew to more than 100 employees. The deal came through the Pentagon's APFIT fast-track program, moving Overland from demos into building at scale, with initial deliveries expected in early 2027.

title: The AI Trade Is Losing One of Its Key Signals
blurb: AI's per-unit pricing is drifting lower, and that matters because it was one of the clearest signals investors used to bet the sector's massive spending would pay off. As markets grow uneasy about whether the sums poured into AI will ever earn a return, falling prices per unit of usage cut against the revenue growth needed to justify the capex. The trade that assumed AI usage would command premium pricing is losing the number it leaned on most.

title: Windsor: Market Has Mistaken Demand for AI Compute
blurb: Semiconductor stocks opened Q3 with their worst two-day selloff in a month, with the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index falling as much as 6% on July 2 after its best quarter ever, an 88% advance. Richard Windsor of Radio Free Mobile argues the market has confused near-term buildout for durable demand, treating a spending cycle as if it were structural. The read for founders building on top of the compute layer is that supplier economics can reprice fast, and infrastructure priced for permanent scarcity rarely stays that way.

title: AI Factories Create Winners and Losers in Power Equipment Market
blurb: The AI factory buildout is splitting the power-equipment market into winners and losers, forcing firms like transformer, switchgear, and grid makers to reshuffle their portfolios around a market projected to top $200B a year. The demand is real, but it rewards the companies positioned for the specific loads AI data centers pull and punishes those still selling for the old grid. The scramble is less about whether power spend grows and more about which slices of the stack capture it.

title: India’s Space Industry Hunts for Its SpaceX Moment
blurb: India's private space sector is showing early traction as the government opens up what was long a state monopoly. A wave of startups is building launch vehicles, satellites, and imaging services on the back of ISRO's low-cost engineering and a newly liberalized regulatory regime. No Indian company has reached SpaceX-scale reusability or cadence yet, but domestic demand for cheap satellite constellations and a growing pool of engineering talent give the field a plausible runway to build a launch industry on its own terms.

title: Trump Says He Wants AI Guardrails, But ‘As Little as Possible’
blurb: Trump signaled he wants federal AI standards but as few as possible, framing heavy regulation as a handicap in the race against China. The read for founders is a lighter-touch federal posture that favors speed over compliance, at least for now. The unresolved question is whether "as little as possible" holds up against state-level rules and safety pressure, or leaves companies navigating a patchwork that ends up costing more than one clear federal standard would.

title: Trump bought Apple, Nvidia and other tech giants before tariff reversal fueled rebound
blurb: Trump made 327 stock purchases on April 8, 2025, more than five times his daily average that year, concentrating on the mega-cap tech names like Apple and Nvidia that his own tariff announcement had just crushed. The next morning he posted "THIS IS A GREAT TIME TO BUY," walked back the tariffs, and the S&P 500 jumped around 9.5% in one session. The benchmark is up roughly 50% since that bottom, and no prior president has held a personal stake this large in markets he can move with a single post.

title: SpaceX may emerge as ultimate blueprint for new wave of mega-cap IPOs
blurb: SpaceX's public debut is becoming the reference case for the mega-cap IPO wave, with Anthropic and OpenAI widely expected to follow the same path. It went public on June 12 at a $2 trillion-plus market cap, ran 53% above its $150 open in three days, then settled to about 17% up, and is already in the Russell 1000 with Nasdaq-100 inclusion set for July 6. The real blueprint is the fast-track index eligibility itself, which pulls fresh passive demand into any name that clears the thresholds. Whether the next debuts repeat it depends on the market they land in.

title: AI search visibility startup geoSurge scores £9.4m round
blurb: geoSurge raised $12M (£9.4m) in an oversubscribed seed round led by AlbionVC to help brands shape how generative AI systems like ChatGPT, Gemini, and Claude understand and remember them. The bet is that AI visibility isn't SEO: instead of tracking real-time citations, geoSurge targets the underlying data models through a "Corpus Engineering" framework meant to influence the representation layer itself. Founded in 2025 with 80% of staff in AI and data science roles, it argues surface-level prompt tracking will commoditize as the real fight moves to how models learn and retain brand information over time.

title: Letter to Scott Bessent: chip group SEMI, which includes Micron and Samsung, warns that US intervention in chip pricing or capacity would worsen chip shortages (Maggie Eastland/Bloomberg)
blurb: SEMI, the chip-industry group whose members include Micron and Samsung, wrote to Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent warning that US intervention in chip pricing or capacity would deepen shortages rather than ease them. The group argues that supply and demand, not government quotas, should set where and how much gets built, and that heavy-handed policy risks distorting an already tight market. For founders whose products depend on silicon, the fight over who controls chip supply is now a Washington question, and the outcome shapes component costs and availability for years.

title: Job seekers giving up: Labor force participation falls to lowest in 50 years
blurb: The June jobs report looks fine until you read the participation number: labor force participation fell to 61.5%, the lowest in 50 years outside the Covid era. Unemployment dropped to 4.2%, but only because 720,000 people left the labor force in a single month, not because hiring picked up. The employment-to-population ratio is now 59%, the lowest since 2021, and economists say this is less a wave of retirements than job seekers giving up. A shrinking pool of workers and spenders is the signal under the headline.

title: Meta caps internal AI token spending
blurb: Meta told roughly 6,000 employees that internal AI usage is on track to cost billions in 2026, after staff burned through 73.7 trillion tokens in about 30 days, much of it on Anthropic's Claude. The company is killing its "Claudeonomics" leaderboard, standing up a centralized AI Gateway dashboard, and rolling out formal token budgets in 2027. The deeper signal for founders is that per-seat AI spend is becoming a real line item worth governing, and even a company spending $135B on infrastructure is now steering employees toward its own cheaper MetaCode assistant.

title: Fintech unicorn GCash to raise $1.5B in potential Philippines record IPO
blurb: GCash operator Mynt is raising up to $1.5B in October in what would be the Philippines' largest IPO, valuing the e-wallet at $10.9B and edging past BDO Unibank, the country's biggest lender. The business behind the headline is real: net income jumped 56% to a record last year on 90 million registered users and 40 million monthly actives. The bigger signal is that a Southeast Asian consumer fintech is now worth more than the incumbent bank it competes with, and its backers, from Ant to Warburg Pincus, are cashing out at the top.

title: Google hit with £3.5bn fine following 8-year battle
blurb: The EU's antitrust case against Google over Android is finally settled, with the top court dismissing Google's appeal and locking in a €4.1B (£3.5bn) fine eight years after the original 2018 ruling. Regulators found Google forced manufacturers to pre-install Search and Chrome and paid for exclusivity, denying rivals a path to compete. That brings Google's EU antitrust bill to almost €11B over the past decade, and it signals that Brussels will keep pursuing platform-default and pre-install tactics that founders building on top of dominant ecosystems now depend on.

title: Finnish quantum computing company IQM closed up 2% in its Nasdaq debut Thursday after going public via a SPAC merger at a ~$1.9B valuation (Anna Heim/TechCrunch)
blurb: Finnish quantum computing company IQM went public on Nasdaq via SPAC merger at a roughly $1.9B valuation, closing up 2% on its debut Thursday. The flat first-day move signals a market pricing quantum on promise rather than revenue, since commercial demand for the hardware is still years out. SPAC listings are also back as a path to public markets for deep-tech companies that can't yet meet the numbers a traditional IPO demands, giving capital-hungry hardware startups a route that had mostly closed after the last SPAC wave collapsed.

title: The promise of AI was a worker who never sleeps, never books leave, and never asks for a raise. The reality is messier: for a growing number of firms, the machine meant to replace payroll is starting to look like a payroll of its own
blurb: Usage-based AI coding tools are quietly turning into a variable payroll line. Uber burned its entire 2026 budget for AI coding assistants in four months as Claude Code spread across engineering, then capped spending at $1,500 per employee a month. The economics invert the pitch: 23% of tech leaders now spend $200 to $500 per developer monthly and 6% of firms spend over $2,000, which at the top end costs more than a junior hire. A tool sold as a tireless worker that never asks for a raise bills by the token, with no natural ceiling.

title: How Sony sold 400 million Walkmans
blurb: In 1979, Sony shipped the Walkman without a record button, ignoring its own market research, its retailers, and its engineers, who all insisted a tape player that couldn't record wouldn't sell. Akio Morita cut the feature anyway and bet his presidency on selling 100,000 units, and the product went on to move 400 million. The lesson isn't to ignore data, it's that a clear point of view about what a product is for can outrank what customers say they want.

title: Weave’s $7,999 Isaac 1 bets home robots don’t need legs or fingers
blurb: Weave Robotics launched Isaac 1, a $7,999 home robot that skips legs and fingers in favor of a wheeled base and two claws, and does laundry-adjacent chores like folding clothes and making beds. The price is the point: bipedal rivals from 1X, Figure, and Unitree run $12,000 to over $20,000 because legs need expensive actuators. Weave is betting that purpose-built machines beat general-purpose humanoids into the home, the same logic pulling billions into physical AI, though the autonomy is still partial and propped up by remote human operators for tricky tasks.

title: Critical Cursor AI Code Editor Flaws Could Lead to OS-Level Remote Code Execution
blurb: Cato Networks disclosed two critical vulnerabilities in Cursor, dubbed DuneSlide (CVE-2026-50548 and CVE-2026-50549, CVSS 9.8), that let a prompt-injected payload escape the IDE sandbox and achieve OS-level remote code execution. Both bugs abuse Cursor's auto-run terminal execution to overwrite the cursorsandbox executable, one via a working_directory allow-list flaw and the other via a symlink path-canonicalization bypass, and were patched in Cursor 3.0.

title: crustc: entirety of `rustc`, translated to C
blurb: crustc translates the entire `rustc` compiler to C, producing a functional Rust compiler you can build with GCC and make against a local LLVM library. It is a showcase for the author's cilly toolchain, which compiles arbitrary Rust to compiler-specific C by generating witness programs that probe what each target's C compiler supports, aimed at old or obscure hardware with no LLVM or GCC backend.

title: Podman v6.0.0
blurb: Podman v6.0.0 modernizes its networking stack, transitioning from slirp4netns and iptables to Netavark, Pasta, and nftables, and adds experimental Pasta rootless port forwarding that preserves source IPs on custom networks. The release also overhauls Quadlet with REST API support and expanded .volume units, upgrades podman machine with multi-provider support and an os update command, and refines Docker API compatibility to ease migration.

title: Vite+ Beta
blurb: VoidZero released the beta of Vite+, a unified web toolchain that wraps Vite 8, Vitest, Rolldown, tsdown, Oxlint, and Oxfmt behind one MIT-licensed CLI with commands like vp dev, vp check, and vp build. It adds a monorepo-aware task runner with caching and manages the runtime and package manager, so tool versions and CI commands stay aligned across a codebase without teams maintaining their own internal platform.

title: AI Writes Faster Than Humans Can Review: A Longitudinal Study of an Enterprise 2x Mandate
blurb: A longitudinal study of an enterprise "2x" mandate tracked 802 developers and 196,212 pull requests from January 2024 to April 2026, finding per-capita merged-PR throughput reached 2.09x the pre-mandate baseline, with a staggered difference-in-differences design tying most of the gain to AI adoption and accumulated usage rather than the mandate itself. Automated review overtook human review as per-reviewer load roughly doubled, while merge and revert rates held steady.

title: CursorBench 3.1
blurb: Cursor released CursorBench 3.1, a benchmark that evaluates coding agents on ambiguous, multi-file tasks pulled from real Cursor sessions, scored on both accuracy and average cost per task. Version 3.1 adds problems targeting codebase understanding, bug-finding, planning, and code review, expanding beyond the edit, refactor, and bugfix tasks in the original 3.0 set.

title: Since Linux 6.9, LUKS suspend stopped wiping disk-encryption keys from memory
blurb: A regression introduced in Linux 6.9 broke `cryptsetup luksSuspend`, which is supposed to flush disk-encryption keys from kernel memory but silently stopped doing so. The bug leaves LUKS keys recoverable from RAM on suspended machines, defeating the exact threat model laptop users rely on suspend-and-lock to cover, and it went unnoticed across multiple stable releases.

title: Android Developer Verification: Threat masquerading as protection
blurb: F-Droid published a sharply critical piece arguing that Google's Android Developer Verification program, which will require all Android developers to register centrally, functions as a control mechanism rather than the anti-malware measure Google frames it as. The post contends that ADV does nothing to stop a bad actor from distributing malware in the first place, and that its real effect is blocking installation of apps from developers Google hasn't approved.

title: Program-as-Weights: A Programming Paradigm for Fuzzy Functions
blurb: This paper proposes fuzzy-function programming, which compiles a natural-language spec into a small locally-executable neural artifact instead of routing every call to an LLM API. A 4B compiler trained on the released 10M-example FuzzyBench emits adapters for a frozen 0.6B Qwen3 interpreter that matches Qwen3-32B prompting at roughly one fiftieth the inference memory, running at 30 tokens/s on a MacBook M3.

title: Coding Agents Are Guessing: Measuring Action-Boundary Violations in Underspecified DevOps Instructions
blurb: UnderSpecBench tests how Claude Code, Codex, and OpenCode behave on 2,208 underspecified DevOps prompts, using side-effect oracles to separate safe actions from wrong-target and over-scope violations. Across five agent-model configurations, 55.8-67.8% of runs crossed at least one action boundary, with the authors showing that underspecification rarely makes agents fail outright and instead makes them guess, especially when the target is ambiguous.

title: Distributed Attacks in Persistent-State AI Control
blurb: This paper introduces Iterative VibeCoding, a benchmark where an untrusted coding agent pursues a covert side task across a sequence of PRs in a persistent codebase, distributing the payload to evade monitors. No single monitor catches both gradual and concentrated attacks, but a stateful link-tracker that tracks suspicious buildup across PRs, combined into a four-monitor ensemble, cuts gradual-attack evasion from 93% to 47%.

title: Adoption and Impact of Command-Line AI Coding Agents: A Study of Microsoft's Early 2026 Rollout of Claude Code and GitHub Copilot CLI
blurb: A study of tens of thousands of Microsoft engineers during its early-2026 rollout of Claude Code and Copilot CLI found that adopters merged roughly 24% more pull requests, a lift that held across a four-month window. First use spread mainly through social networks and retention tracked coding activity rather than demographics, leading the authors to argue that organizations should treat visible peer use as central to rollout strategy.

title: Vulnerability and Malware Checks in uv
blurb: Astral added two preview security features to uv: `uv audit`, a native alternative to `pip-audit` that scans dependencies for known vulnerabilities and deprecated packages while running 4x to 10x faster by reusing uv's locked resolutions, and an opt-in OSV malware lookup on every sync. The malware check runs on `uv add` and `uv sync` when you set `UV_MALWARE_CHECK=1`, flagging previously-resolved malicious packages before they land in your environment.

title: Regression Accumulation in Multi-Turn LLM Programming Conversations
blurb: This paper studies regression accumulation in multi-turn LLM coding sessions, where later suggestions break requirements from earlier turns. Across 26,016 turn instances on six models, 40% to 73% of tasks lost previously correct behavior over an 8-turn chain, with cross-turn conflict the dominant failure class and a verification gate that rolls back against prior tests the only fix that consistently helped, lifting DeepSeek-V3 from 75.8% to 87.9%.

title: How GitHub used secret scanning to reach inbox zero
blurb: GitHub found 20,000+ secret scanning alerts across 15,000 repositories, but discovered that just five repos accounted for roughly 18,000 of them, all inactive test fixtures and fake-but-valid-looking credentials. The post details how the security team separated signal from noise, tracked down live secrets in support tickets and other non-code locations, assigned ownership, and drove open alerts to zero over nine months.

title: Decoupling Code Complexity from Newcomer Participation: A Causal Study of AI Coding Agent Adoption in OSS
blurb: A causal study of 1,888 GitHub projects that adopted an AI coding agent finds no evidence that tools like Cursor and Claude Code crowd out newcomers, with newcomer inflow, onboarding, and retention unchanged after adoption. Adoption did raise per-function complexity (about +11% on a cognitive metric for Python, +3 to 4% cyclomatic across languages), but in the subsets where complexity rose, newcomer participation held steady.

title: iOS 27 helps apps detect when a user may be getting scammed in real time
blurb: Apple is adding Trust Insights to iOS 27, a mostly on-device framework that flags when a user may be getting coached through a social engineering scam across payments, account changes, and messaging. It analyzes interaction patterns, timing, and sensor data locally, discards the raw signals, and returns a medium or high risk level apps can use to add warnings, delays, or extra verification steps.

title: Beyond Textual Repository Exploration: Dual-Modal Structural Reasoning for Agentic Issue Resolution
blurb: DUALVIEW gives issue-resolution agents four persistent graph views of a repository (module coupling, function call, class hierarchy, and program dependence) exposed through a queryable visual-and-textual interface. On SWE-bench Pro and Verified, this dual-modal scaffolding improves resolution across agent architectures and model families, with ablations crediting the visual externalization of dependencies for better long-horizon exploration.

title: Reasoning effort, not tool access, buys first-try reliability in agentic code generation: an observational study
blurb: An observational study ran 90 agent builds of the same app from one spec and found capability tier, not extra tooling, drove first-try reliability. Raising reasoning effort from High to xHigh lifted perfect first runs from 28% to 89% for 9-29% more cost, while a browser testing tool added 42-68% cost without improving function.

title: Lightning Memory-Mapped Database Manager (LMDB) 1.0
blurb: LMDB 1.0 shipped, a Btree-based embedded key/value store that exposes the entire database as a memory map so reads return pointers directly with no malloc, memcpy, or page cache layer. It offers full ACID transactions with lockless readers via multi-version copy-on-write, requires no write-ahead log or compaction, and reuses free pages so the file does not grow without bound.

title: Steerability via constraints: a substrate for scalable oversight of coding agents
blurb: The authors argue that the decades-old playbook for managing large human engineering teams (access control, network policies, tooling-enforced conventions) transfers directly to coding agents and costs fewer tokens than elaborate agentic scaffolding. In their controlled test, a small Gemma 4 reviewer caught 90.9% of 11 inserted Python backdoors under a constrained substrate plus a ~200-LoC docs CLI, up from 54.5% unconstrained, with substrate and tooling contributing independently.

title: Underspecification does not imply Incoherence: The Risks of Semantic Collapse in Coding Models
blurb: This paper challenges the assumption that LLMs handle ambiguous coding prompts by producing varied outputs, showing instead that models often collapse onto a single incorrect interpretation and generate coherent but behaviorally misaligned code. The authors call this detrimental semantic collapse and measure it in over 10% of MBPP tasks, 3% of HumanEval, and 32% of LiveCodeBench, with rates rising more than 5x when underspecification is injected deliberately.

title: Prompt Coverage Adequacy
blurb: This paper proposes Prompt Coverage Adequacy, a test-adequacy criterion for LLM-generated code that measures how well a test suite exercises the requirements expressed in a prompt by leveraging the model's attention mechanism. Evaluated across two datasets and multiple LLMs, an attention-boosting instantiation uncovered over 30% more faults than traditional code coverage when used to guide test generation.

title: Understanding Agent-Based Patching of Compiler Missed Optimizations
blurb: This paper benchmarks how well coding agents patch LLVM missed optimizations, finding that agents typically fix the reported example but frequently under-cover, partially overlap, or overshoot the developer-intended optimization scope. The authors introduce historical-knowledge augmentation that retrieves and distills prior LLVM optimization pull requests, which improves developer-aligned generalization and yields practical gains on real-world IR.

title: Immich 3.0
blurb: Immich 3.0 is the latest major release of the open-source, self-hosted photo and video backup platform that serves as a privacy-focused alternative to Google Photos. The project bundles mobile apps for iOS and Android, automatic backup from the phone camera roll, machine-learning-based face and object search, and multi-user support, all running on your own hardware via Docker.

title: Vercel's Andrew Qu on why agents are a new kind of software
blurb: In this Latent Space interview, Vercel's Andrew Qu argues that agents are a new kind of software, born from the paper cuts his team hit building v0: model switching, provider fallbacks, and resumable runs that existing tooling did not handle. He explains how those fixes hardened into eve, Vercel's agent framework, and why skills, sandboxes, and agent-readable websites now shape how software gets built.

title: KRCA: An Efficient Root Cause Analysis System in Hyper-Scale Microservice Systems via Agentic AI
blurb: KRCA is an end-to-end root cause analysis system for hyper-scale microservice systems that chains an API-level drilldown, a skeleton-based causal graph, and a memory-augmented multi-agent framework to localize failing services and classify failure types. Deployed at Kuaishou for over six months, it hits AC@1 scores of 0.88 and 0.79, beats the strongest baseline by at least 31% in absolute gains, and cut average diagnosis time by 77.3%.

title: The short leash AI coding method for beating Fable
blurb: The author argues that expert developers should keep AI agents on a "short leash," reviewing and steering every step rather than delegating to orchestrated fleets of parallel agents that produce unreadable, inefficient code. The piece contends that vibe-coding workflows make it impossible to build real understanding of a codebase, and that for security-critical software, tight human oversight beats even frontier-model review.

title: How OpenAI Delivers Low-Latency Voice AI for 900M Users
blurb: OpenAI serves voice AI to 900M weekly users on WebRTC, splitting the stack into a stateless edge relay for packet routing and a stateful transceiver that owns the heavy WebRTC state. The relay reads the ICE ufrag off the first packet as a routing key, sidestepping the standard SFU model since nearly all traffic is one user talking to one model rather than multiparty calls.

title: Rethinking Complexity Metrics for LLM-Integrated Applications: Beyond Source Code
blurb: HECATE is a tool that measures complexity in both the prompt and code layers of LLM-integrated applications, treating each prompt as a Hoare-logic-style behavioral specification rather than opaque text. Testing 52 candidate metrics against maintenance history from 18 repos, the authors found only ten survive once code size is controlled, and their prompt-layer metrics predict maintenance effort independently of any code-level metric.

title: Visual Studio Code improves tools for agents
blurb: VS Code 1.127 makes browser tools for agents generally available, letting agents open pages in the integrated browser to read content, capture console errors, take screenshots, and navigate to verify their own work. The release also adds per-site browser permissions for APIs like camera and USB, groupable agent session lists, and hover-to-view AI credit costs for delegated subagent work.

title: Mitigating Package Hallucinations in Large Language Models via Model Editing
blurb: This paper introduces BOUND, a localized model-editing framework that uses risk-aware localization plus lightweight LoRA adapters to stop LLMs from recommending non-existent package names, a hallucination attackers exploit by registering malicious packages under those names. BOUND cuts the package-level hallucination rate by 79.9% on edit prompts and 65.4% on unseen ones, and the learned validity boundary generalizes, reducing hallucinations 34.0% in pip install recommendations and 12.8% in code generation.

title: Postgres transactions are a distributed systems superpower
blurb: The DBOS team argues that co-locating your durable workflow state in the same Postgres database as your application data lets both be updated in a single transaction, eliminating partial failures. The post shows how this collapses hard problems like idempotency and atomicity into ordinary transactional steps, dropping the usual application-level bookkeeping such as a separate applied_payments table used to guard against double-crediting on retry.

title: Show HN: ZeroFS – A log-structured filesystem for S3
blurb: ZeroFS serves S3-compatible buckets as POSIX filesystems over NFS and 9P and as raw block devices over NBD, all from a single userspace process that writes immutable compressed, encrypted segments and serves warm reads from local cache. The bundled root-less FUSE client is the recommended Linux mount, and the project reports passing 8,662 pjdfstest cases, xfstests, parallel kernel builds, and Jepsen consistency checks including a ZFS mirror across three S3 regions.

title: Launch HN: Manufact (YC S25) – MCP Cloud
blurb: Manufact (YC S25) launched MCP Cloud, a platform that auto-deploys MCP apps and servers from a GitHub push and ships them to the ChatGPT Apps Store and Claude Connectors from one codebase. It bundles the open-source mcp-use SDK, per-branch preview URLs, a browser-based Cloud Inspector for tracing JSON-RPC and swapping models, and production analytics with session replay.

title: Too many tables are bad for you
blurb: The Cybertec team benchmarks how a Postgres schema with tens of thousands of tables degrades performance, tracing the slowdown to bloated system catalogs, autovacuum churning through every relation, and slower planning as the catalog cache thrashes. The post quantifies the hit across connection startup, `pg_dump`, and autovacuum cycles, and argues that partitioning sprawl or multi-tenant table-per-tenant designs are the usual culprits worth consolidating.

title: File-Level Copying Is an Implicit Dependency in Open Source
blurb: This paper studies file-level copying across open source using World of Code, extracting 690,500 copy events to show that copying strips the provenance, maintenance, security, and compliance signals a package manager provides. Copied sources are usually stale and rarely record their origin, and the resulting risk reaches packaged software invisibly to dependency scanners, with 17,314 CVE-risk vendored copies and 41,777 license-reuse candidates.

title: Claude-real-video － any LLM can watch a video
blurb: claude-real-video is a local Python tool that extracts video frames for any LLM to analyze, pulling frames at every scene change rather than a fixed interval and discarding near-duplicates before handing off a folder of images, an audio transcript, and a manifest. It runs entirely on your machine via yt-dlp, ffmpeg, and Whisper, feeding models fewer but more meaningful frames than Gemini's default 1 fps sampling for cheaper context and better comprehension.

title: How To Corrupt An SQLite Database File
blurb: The SQLite documentation catalogs the concrete ways a database file goes corrupt despite SQLite's automatic rollback, from processes overwriting the file with garbage to stale file descriptors leaking writes into a reopened database. A notable case hit the Fossil DVCS repository when stderr was closed before sqlite3_open_v2(), leaving fd 2 pointing at the database so a later assert() error message wrote directly into it, which motivated SQLite 3.8.1 refusing low-numbered file descriptors.

title: Inside Thinking Machines' Interaction Models
blurb: Thinking Machines argues that the standard voice-AI stack, where a turn-based language model is wrapped in helper systems for pause detection, transcription, and speech synthesis, has a hard ceiling because the model can only perceive input once the user stops talking. Their proposed interaction model replaces the hand-off-and-wait pattern with continuous collaboration, letting the human clarify, redirect, and give feedback while the model is still working.

title: Using LLMs to Analyze Spark SQL Plans: A Practical Approach to Debugging Long-Running Jobs
blurb: The Expedia engineering team built a tool that feeds Spark SQL physical plans to an LLM to diagnose why long-running jobs stall, surfacing issues like skewed joins, missing partition filters, and excessive shuffles. The post covers how they parse the plan output into structured input, prompt the model for concrete bottleneck explanations, and fold the results into their existing debugging workflow rather than replacing manual tuning.

title: In defense of AI mandates (xpost)
blurb: Charity Majors argues that AI mandates, despised as they are, function as a legitimate funding mechanism for coordinated change, since they give managers explicit cover for slipped deadlines and lower quality while teams build new skills. Her point is that refusing to mandate is the same as telling engineers the shift is not a priority and must happen in their spare time, leaving the change unfunded and unowned.

title: Understanding Build Reproducibility in the F-Droid Ecosystem
blurb: This paper presents the first empirical study of build reproducibility in F-Droid, rebuilding 18,904 Android app versions that had been confirmed bitwise reproducible between September 2018 and February 2026 and hitting an 83% rebuild success rate. Missing dependencies caused 76% of failures, and 94% of the apps that did rebuild remained bitwise identical, showing that rebuildability decays over time even when reproducibility itself holds.

title: Show HN: CLI tool for detecting non-exact code duplication with embedding models
blurb: Slopo is a CLI tool that detects non-exact code duplication by embedding each code unit and flagging pairs whose vectors sit close together, targeting similar snippets scattered across modules that copy-paste detectors and humans miss. It supports Python, TypeScript, JavaScript, Java, Kotlin, C#, Go, and Rust, installs via uv, and outputs ranked clusters meant to be fed to an AI agent for confirmation and refactoring.

title: Fedora 45 could get Shadow Stack support to prevent ROP attacks
blurb: Fedora 45 may ship with Shadow Stack enabled by default, adding hardware-backed protection against return-oriented programming attacks on CPUs that support the feature. Shadow Stack keeps a protected copy of return addresses and traps any mismatch, blocking the ROP technique of chaining existing code gadgets to bypass non-executable memory defenses.

title: The primary purpose of code review is to find code that will be hard to maintain
blurb: The author argues that code review's main value is catching code that will be hard to maintain, not finding bugs or verifying correctness, since maintainability is the thing authors are worst at judging about their own work. Bugs surface through tests and production, but a reviewer reading the code cold is uniquely positioned to flag what a future maintainer will struggle to understand.

title: No LLM Code in Dependencies
blurb: The maintainer of git-annex spent about 100 hours over the past month verifying the project can build without dependencies containing LLM-generated code, citing reverted large-scale changes, an incoherent 1,489-line commit message, and prompts that copied code from other projects. The author argues the practice forces ongoing manual review of the entire dependency tree and is reconsidering participation in communities that won't police AI-generated contributions.

title: Immich 3.0 released with non-destructive photo editing, automated Workflows and more
blurb: Immich 3.0, the self-hosted Google Photos alternative, adds non-destructive photo editing that preserves originals, plus automated Workflows for routing and tagging assets without manual sorting. The release also improves video playback and background syncing on mobile alongside a batch of stability fixes across the web and native apps.

title: Query Faster, Query Smarter: Our Move to DuckDB and What We Learned
blurb: Arcesium migrated thousands of SQL queries across three engines over 18 months, moving Athena to Trino to DuckDB after Athena's account-level rate limits caused roughly 40% of their incidents and a client onboarding pushed them to 10x scale. Switching to DuckDB's in-memory OLAP engine, accessed from Java over JDBC against Parquet files in S3, cut runtime and resources by 50% each and halved query costs.

title: How We Built DEmate: Taming LLMs for Data Engineering at Meta
blurb: Meta detailed how it built DEmate, an internal LLM-powered assistant that automates data engineering work like pipeline authoring, debugging, and schema navigation across its large-scale data stack. The write-up covers how the team constrained model outputs against Meta's proprietary tooling and metadata to reduce hallucinations, and the guardrails and evaluation loops needed to make LLM suggestions reliable enough for production data engineers.

title: TestEvo-Bench: An Executable and Live Benchmark for Test and Code Co-Evolution
blurb: TestEvo-Bench is an executable, continuously-updated benchmark for test and code co-evolution, with test-generation and test-update tracks mined from real Java commit histories and scored on pass rate, coverage, and mutation score. Its 1,255 tasks carry timestamps so evaluation can exclude anything predating a model's training cutoff, and top agents scored up to 77.5%, dropping sharply on recent tasks and tight cost budgets.

title: Local Reasoning for Global Properties
blurb: The author argues that current AI generates strong local code but stumbles on tasks requiring global program understanding, showing up as a proliferation of defensive checks that explode the number of states future readers must reason about. The post asks whether programming language design, which lets local reasoning guarantee surprising global properties, could once again help if that limitation proves durable.

title: Single header Parser Combinators for C
blurb: CParseC is a single-header, dependency-free C99 library that brings Haskell Parsec-style composable parser combinators to C, using macros instead of function pointers to stay inlining-friendly and zero-copy with a user-supplied arena. Its benchmark CSV parser runs about 1.25x faster than Rust's BurntSushi/rust-csv and roughly 20x faster than attoparsec-csv, with optional SIMD-specialized combinators and no hidden allocations.

title: The GNU Emacs Architecture: Unlocking the Core [pdf]
blurb: A master's thesis dissecting GNU Emacs internals, covering its Lisp interpreter core, buffer model, and the C-to-Elisp boundary that makes it endlessly extensible.

title: Building Indexes on a Moving Target
blurb: Apache Hudi can build a new metadata index (record-level, secondary, expression, column stats, or bloom filter) over a live multi-petabyte table without pausing ingestion, draining pipelines, or scheduling a swap window. The bootstrap reads historical state into a fresh metadata partition while concurrent writers route new mutations into the same file groups, and a final reconciliation step stitches the seam, an approach that depends on Hudi's append-first mutation model.

title: Understanding Software Defect Prediction: A Large-scale Empirical Study Across Uncertainty Quantification and Performance Evaluation
blurb: Large-scale study finds software defect prediction uncertainty metrics are context-dependent and rarely transfer across projects.

title: Show HN: ZeroFS – A log-structured filesystem for S3
blurb: ZeroFS is a log-structured filesystem that serves S3-compatible buckets as POSIX filesystems over NFS and 9P, and as raw block devices over NBD, all from a single userspace process. Writes upload as immutable segments that are compressed and encrypted, warm reads come from local cache, and the project ships tested against 8,662 pjdfstest cases plus xfstests, kernel builds, and Jepsen histories.

title: Self-Gating Attention for Efficient Time Series Forecasting
blurb: This paper proposes Self-Gating Attention (SGA), a plug-and-play replacement for self-attention in time series forecasting that drops the query and key projections in favor of a shared learnable matrix plus an input-dependent residual. The result is linear time and memory in look-back length instead of quadratic, since attention maps across timestamps turn out to be highly redundant in real-world series. Across nine datasets spanning electricity, finance, weather, and climate, SGA cuts inference cost while matching state-of-the-art forecasting accuracy.

title: HNSW with Accuracy Guarantees Using Graph Spanners -- A Technical Report
blurb: This paper proposes "Certify-then-Rectify," a framework that keeps HNSW's fast greedy search but adds a distribution-free statistical certifier to flag low-quality results and escalate to exact recovery only when needed. To make that fallback affordable, the authors reinterpret the HNSW graph as a geometric spanner and use Extreme Value Theory to bound the true nearest-neighbor distance. The result gives HNSW's average-case speed with worst-case correctness guarantees that vector search has historically lacked.

title: Postgres transactions are a distributed systems superpower
blurb: The DBOS team argues that workflow state and application data should live in the same Postgres database, not separate stores, because co-location lets both be updated in a single transaction. That eliminates partial failures: idempotency problems like double-crediting a bank account disappear when a step's side effect and its checkpoint commit atomically, removing the need for application-level bookkeeping tables like applied_payments. The pitch is that Postgres transactions do the hard distributed-systems work for free.

title: From Single to Multiple Attributes: Experimental Insights on Sampling-Based Distinct Combination Estimation in GROUP-BY Queries
blurb: This paper tackles distinct-combination cardinality estimation for multi-attribute GROUP-BY queries, a case existing estimators mostly ignore since prior work targets SPJ queries or single-attribute distinct-value counts. The authors build a workload generator for filtered and non-filtered GROUP-BY queries, test sampling-based and learned methods across four real datasets and TPC-H, and trace how estimation errors propagate into PostgreSQL's plan choices. The takeaway is that sample joint-distribution info alone is often insufficient, and current methods underuse single-attribute signals they could exploit.

title: AgenticDataBench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Data Agents
blurb: AgenticDataBench introduces a benchmark for LLM-based data agents, spanning 15 vertical domains and including five real-world B2B tasks from a fintech company, with fine-grained ground-truth labels scored at the level of individual data science skills. Those skills are recurring operational patterns mined from Stack Overflow solutions via hierarchical clustering, which lets the authors quantify coverage and report where state-of-the-art agents actually break down rather than a single pass-rate number.

title: CADENZA in Action: Breaking the Monolith with Intent-Dependent Plan Spaces for Semantic Queries
blurb: CADENZA is a semantic query optimizer that compiles a natural-language intent into decomposed steps, picks a physical implementation for each, and tunes parameters against user-set quality, latency, and cost targets. Instead of treating an LLM or embedding model as a monolithic operator, it breaks the intent into a plan space where cheaper components can handle sub-steps that don't need a full model call. The demo lets users watch how different preference weightings produce different winning plans over multimodal databases.

title: The Semantic Layer Blackhole: From Initial Build to Perpetual Governance
blurb: The author argues that building a semantic layer is finite, bounded work, while maintaining one is a different category of problem that compounds combinatorially as the business changes underneath it. Redefining a shared entity like "customer" ripples across every metric built on it, so N metrics over M shared entities create roughly N×M relationships a single change can touch, with both growing every quarter. The hardest part is social, not technical: getting teams to delete their local KPI hacks once a shared definition exists.

title: Matching AI Modality To User Intent: Designing The Right Interface
blurb: The author argues that the design industry's default of routing every AI capability through a chat bubble ignores modality, and that input and output methods should be matched to a user's physical context and cognitive load. The piece offers two practical tools, a Task Audit and an Input/Output Alignment Matrix, for deciding when voice, visual, or touch beats text for a given task.

title: SpaceDNA arranges split levels of Indian home around rust-orange staircase
blurb: SpaceDNA completed Splitscape Residence, a multi-generational house on a 113-square-metre site in Coimbatore, organized across split levels and mezzanines that wind around a rust-orange, oxide-coated staircase and a triple-height void. Perforated steel screens filter daylight through the three-bedroom home, a compact demonstration of how vertical stacking and a single circulation spine can open up a tight urban plot.

title: When Do LLM Personas Support Visualization Design? A Cross-Model Study of Color Assignment and Chart Choice
blurb: A cross-model study tested whether Big Five personas conditioned on GPT-4o-mini, GPT-4.1-mini, and GPT-5-mini produce stable color-assignment and chart-choice preferences, and found the personality signal is largely an artifact of model choice and task framing rather than a reliable effect. The authors conclude that LLM personas work as exploratory probes for early visualization design, not as substitutes for real users, and urge multi-model testing and no-persona baselines.

title: Cubo arranges "unmistakably Lego" innovation centre around 25‑tonne brick
blurb: Cubo Arkitekter completed Kornmarken Campus, an innovation and production centre on Lego's Billund campus built from hybrid mass timber and concrete around a 25-tonne brick centrepiece. The building houses Lego's innovation teams and joins BIG's Lego House and the company's headquarters nearby, extending the toymaker's pattern of turning its own architecture into brand-legible landmarks.

title: The website of the future may assemble itself for every visitor
blurb: Adobe is experimenting with "agentic sites" that assemble a page around each visitor's inferred intent in real time, retrieving from a company's existing content rather than inventing an experience from scratch. Principal Scientist Carlos Sanchez calls it an "audience of one," where browsing signals sort a user into an intent category like exploring or purchasing and an LLM reorganizes copy, product selection, and layout to match.

title: Getty Plans to Terminate Shutterstock Merger Because of UK Regulations
blurb: Getty Images announced it will terminate its $3.7B merger with Shutterstock after the UK's Competition and Markets Authority demanded Shutterstock divest its editorial imaging business, a condition Getty's board unanimously rejected. The collapse strands both stock agencies as they fight generative AI's erosion of the licensing market, and Shutterstock's shares fell roughly 30% on the news, down 48% year to date.

title: MAD adds trio of "cloud buildings" to Tencent headquarters in Shenzhen
blurb: MAD completed the Tengyun Center for Tencent in Shenzhen, a 72,000-square-metre complex of three glazed "cloud" volumes linked by steel trusses alongside two office towers. The project anchors Tencent's 80-hectare headquarters across Qianhai and Da Chan Bay, extending the tech-campus-as-landmark pattern that pairs signature architecture with corporate scale.

title: Physical surfaces make touch interactions in virtual reality precise, efficient, and bimanual
blurb: A study tested three levels of haptic feedback for precise VR touch tasks like selecting, tracing, and sketching, and found that a portable physical surface beat both vibrotactile feedback and visual-only interaction on precision, efficiency, and sketch quality. Participants also used both hands more when grounded against a real surface, reporting greater confidence and control than with mid-air gestures.

title: Critical Cursor AI Code Editor Flaws Could Lead to OS-Level Remote Code Execution
blurb: Two critical flaws in the Cursor AI code editor, tracked as CVE-2026-50548 and CVE-2026-50549 (CVSS 9.8) and dubbed DuneSlide, allow prompt-injection payloads to escape the IDE sandbox and achieve OS-level RCE via working-directory allow-list abuse and a symlink path-canonicalization bypass. Cato Networks reported the bugs in February and Cursor patched both in version 3.0, released April 2, so teams should confirm developer endpoints are upgraded past that build.

title: FBI Seizes NetNut Proxy Platform, Popa Botnet
blurb: The FBI seized hundreds of domains tied to NetNut, a residential proxy service run by Israeli firm Alarum Technologies (NASDAQ: ALAR), which security firms linked to the Popa botnet of at least two million compromised smart TVs, streaming boxes, and other home devices. Defenders should treat NetNut exit-node IPs as hostile, since Google's GTIG observed 316 distinct threat-actor clusters using them for password spraying and origin masking in a single week in June 2026.

title: CISA: Microsoft SharePoint RCE flaw now actively exploited
blurb: CISA added CVE-2026-45659, a high-severity deserialization RCE in Microsoft SharePoint Server, to its KEV catalog after confirming active exploitation. The low-complexity flaw lets any authenticated user with Site Member permissions execute code remotely without user interaction; Microsoft patched it on May 21, and FCEB agencies must remediate by Saturday under BOD 26-04, with Shadowserver tracking over 10,000 exposed servers.

title: New CitrixBleed Vulnerability Exploited Immediately After Public Disclosure
blurb: A new CitrixBleed-style flaw in NetScaler ADC and Gateway (CVE-2026-8451, CVSS 8.8), an out-of-bounds read in the XML parser that leaks memory via the NSC_TASS cookie on appliances configured as SAML IDP, was exploited within 24 hours of its June 30 disclosure and watchTowr's technical writeup. Patch immediately or disable SAML IDP, and hunt for exploitation by inspecting /saml/login traffic and NSC_TASS cookie values in logs.

title: ConsentFix and ClickFix: How Microsoft 365 Accounts are Hijacked in 3 Seconds
blurb: ConsentFix is a new social-engineering variant that hijacks Microsoft 365 accounts by tricking users into dragging a localhost callback link into their browser during a spoofed OAuth consent flow, surrendering session tokens in roughly three seconds without exploiting any vulnerability. Delivered via trusted platforms like Dropbox and DocSend, it evades traditional awareness training, so defenders should restrict OAuth app consent, alert on anomalous token grants, and enforce conditional-access session controls.

title: Since Linux 6.9, LUKS suspend stopped wiping disk-encryption keys from memory
blurb: A regression introduced in Linux 6.9 broke `cryptsetup luksSuspend`, which since then no longer wipes LUKS disk-encryption master keys from kernel memory as intended. The failure leaves keys recoverable in RAM while a laptop is suspended, defeating the cold-boot and evil-maid protections that luksSuspend exists to provide and exposing full-disk encryption on any affected machine seized in a suspended state.

title: Microsoft said exploitation was 'less likely' ... but CISA just added SharePoint RCE to KEV list
blurb: CISA added a Microsoft SharePoint remote code execution flaw to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog, confirming active in-the-wild exploitation despite Microsoft's original assessment that abuse was "less likely." On-prem SharePoint remains the exposure: Redmond's initial patch failed to fully remediate the bug, leaving internet-facing servers open to unauthenticated RCE, so defenders should apply the follow-up fix and hunt for compromise rather than assume patched appliances are clean.

title: Cisco finally confirms attackers exploiting Unified CM flaw
blurb: Cisco confirmed active exploitation of CVE-2026-20230, an unauthenticated SSRF flaw in Unified Communications Manager patched on June 3, after Defused and SSD Secure documented in-the-wild attacks using file:// payloads to write files on targeted devices. Admins who can't immediately move to Unified CM 14SU6 or 15SU5 should disable the WebDialer service; Shadowserver tracks over 200 exposed instances, mostly in Asia and North America.

title: Smooth AI criminal drives 'first' end-to-end agentic ransomware attack
